3.2: Credit Proposal Tracking System (CPTS)
Applicability All Scheduled Commercial Banks (excluding Regional Rural Banks)
Explanation Credit Proposal Tracking System (CPTS): Banks are required to put in place a CPTS/ equivalent 
tracking mechanism to facilitate central registration and a system of e-tracking of all MSME loan 
applications. The Credit Proposal Tracking System (CPTS) of the banks may ensure that:
• All MSME applications received by the branch physically with required documents are 
entered in CPTS.
• CPTS shall automatically generate an acknowledgement, having a unique application serial 
number.
• This acknowledgement shall be issued by the branch to the applicant.
• Banks should endeavor to make application status available to the customer online using 
the unique application serial number on a day to day basis.
• The acknowledgement and status of the application to be sent automatically to the applicant
• On submission of set of documents as per check list, the disposal time of the application 
should also be advised to the applicant, with banks right to demand additional documents 
/ information as it feel necessary.
As an example, the CPTS system of Bank of India is presented below: 
(https://bankofindia.co.in/documents/20121/205926/MSME_Policy_2024.pdf)
o USER ID:
o Password:
o For adding new application details in the module, branches should select: Add new Item.
o Branches should enter all the relevant data pertaining to the applicant and then shall advise 
the generated application ID to the applicant.
o For editing the existing record, branches should click on Edit Item. Thereafter branches 
may select the name of the concerned borrower for whom any editing is required.
o Under Edit item branches update the status of the proposal processing 
o Updating the status as per the processing stages of the loan applications.
o Every stage of loan application i.e. from the entry of application details from branch till 
the disposal of the application (pending/sanctioned/rejected) is to be updated from time to 
time by the parent branch, which has received the application from the borrower.
o MIS of total applications shall be made available to both Zonal Office and Head office or 
proper monitoring and control
Checking the Application Status:
Applicants can track the status of  loan application by visiting following  link:
https://www.bankofindia.co.in/engllsh/Track-SME-loan-status.aspx
After logging, on the page the applicant needs to put in his application ID and the status of his 
application will be displayed.

3.3: Digital Lending
Applicability All Commercial Banks, All Primary (Urban) Co-operative Banks, State Co-operative Banks, Central Co-
operative Banks,
All Non-Banking Financial Companies (including Housing Finance Companies), and All All-India Financial 
Institutions.
Referred to as a Regulated Entity (RE) and collectively as Regulated Entities (REs)
Explanation Digital Lending: A remote and automated lending process, largely by use of seamless digital technologies for 
customer acquisition, credit assessment, loan approval, disbursement, recovery, and associated customer 
service.
Digital Lending Apps/ Platforms (DLAs): Mobile and/or web-based applications, on a standalone basis or as 
a part of suite of functions of an application with user interface that facilitate digital lending services. DLAs 
shall include applications of the RE as well as those operated by Lending Service Provider (LSP) engaged by 
RE for extending any credit facilitation services in conformity with extant outsourcing guidelines issued by 
the Reserve Bank.
Lending Service Provider (LSP): An agent of a RE (including another RE) who carries out one or more of RE’s 
digital lending functions, or part thereof, in customer acquisition, services incidental to underwriting and 
pricing, servicing, monitoring, recovery of specific loan or loan portfolio on behalf of RE in conformity with 
extant outsourcing guidelines issued by the Reserve Bank
Digital lending activities of the REs are as follows:
i. RE shall obtain the necessary information relating to economic profile of the borrower with a view to 
assessing the borrower’s creditworthiness before extending any loan, including, at a minimum, age, 
occupation and income details. 
ii. RE shall provide a Key Fact Statement (KFS)
iii. RE shall ensure that digitally signed documents (on the letter head of the RE) viz., KFS, summary of 
loan product, sanction letter, terms and conditions, account statements, privacy policies of the RE / LSP 
with respect to storage and usage of borrowers’ data, etc. shall automatically flow to the borrower on 
the registered and verified email/ SMS upon execution of the loan contract/ transactions.
iv. RE shall maintain a website of their own in public domain, which shall be kept up to date, inter-alia, 
with the following details at a prominent single place on the website for ease of accessibility:
 (a) Details of all of its digital lending products and its DLAs;
 (b) Details of LSPs and the DLAs of the LSPs along with the details of the activities for which they have 
been engaged for;
 (c) Particulars of RE’s customer care and internal grievance redressal mechanism;
 (d) Link to RBI’s Complaint Management System (CMS) and Sachet Portal;
v. Cooling -off Period: The borrower shall be given an explicit option to exit a digital loan by paying the 
principal and the proportionate APR without any penalty during an initial “cooling-off period”. The 
cooling off period shall be determined by the Board of the RE as laid down in their loan policy, subject 
to the period so determined not being less than one day. For borrower continuing with the loan even 
after cooling-off period, pre-payment shall continue to be allowed as per applicable RBI guidelines8.
vi. RE may retain a reasonable one-time processing fee, if the customer exits the loan during the cooling-
off period. This, if applicable, shall be disclosed to the customer upfront in KFS.
vii. Precautions to be taken by MSME while availing credit facility from the Fintech Lenders:
 a) Verify the credibility of the fintech lenders
 b) Understand the loan terms completely
 c) Check the hidden charges (like tech fee, documentation charges, insurance bundling)
 d) Avoid over borrowing
 e) Check the repayment structure
 f) Review the digital loan agreement
 g) Avoid unlicensed loan apps.
 h) Check grievance redressal mechanism
 i) Avoid rollover and refinancing terms
 j) Only share data via secure, encrypted platforms.

RBI/2025-26/36

3.4:  Access to Credit through Straight Through Processing (STP)
 Straight through Processing (Shishu Mudra)
Applicability Commercial Banks, Regional Rural Banks, Small Finance Banks, Urban Co-operative Banks and 
NBFCs
Explanation Straight through Processing (Shishu Mudra) for Existing Customers-Up to Rs 50,000
STP is a fully digital, end-to-end automated loan processing system. There is no manual 
intervention and as such it is free from any discretion.
STP enables:
o Instant KYC verification via Aadhaar/OTP
o Auto-populated forms from UIDAI or PAN
o Direct credit assessments using credit bureau data
o Faster sanction and disbursal without branch visits

4.1: Credit Information Companies
Applicability All Lending Agencies
Explanation Credit Information companies (CICs) institutions that collect and maintain credit-related 
information about individuals and businesses, which is then used to generate credit reports 
and scores. These reports are crucial for lenders to assess the creditworthiness of borrowers.
In India CICs, are regulated by RBI under the Credit Information Companies (Regulation) Act, 
2005 (CICRA 2005).
There are four major CICs: TransUnion CIBIL, Experian, Equifax and CRIF High Mark 
o CICs gather data from various sources, including public records, credit transactions, and 
payment histories from lending institutions.
o Based on the collected data, CICs create Credit Information Reports
o CICs also generate credit scores.
o Lenders use these credit reports and scores to make informed decisions about loan 
applications, and other credit-related services. 
A credit score is a three-digit number that banks/ financial institutions used to decide 
whether a potential borrower is qualified to borrow a loan, banks and financial institutions.
Factors used for Credit Score Calculation:
1. Payment History: Record of timely payments
2. Credit Utilization: amount used relative to total available credit
3. Total number of accounts -types of credit facilities availed
4. Age of Credit: the tenure of credit history
Impact of these factors while calculating credit score:
o Payment History – High Impact
o Credit Utilisation Ratio – High Impact
o Total Number of Accounts – Low Impact
o Age of Credit – Medium Impact
Example: CIBIL Credit score range (This is an indicative list of credit scores)
CIBIL Credit Score Range Credit Rating
750-900 Excellent
650-750 Good

Lower the credit score, higher will be the cost of credit.

Applicability All lending Agencies

India (SEBI) under the SEBI (Credit Rating Agencies) Regulations, 1999. 
 A credit rating agency assigns a rating to various entities as borrowers based on a range of 
metrics and factors. Ratings are given in the form of letters such as AAA, CCC.

& Research (CARE)

 • Credit ratings are based on a number of factors, including market-based, historically 
estimated information at the corporate level. The assessments span from business 
attributes to underlying investments, and they are all intended to assess the 
borrower’s ability to repay its debt.
 • The credit rating by external rating agencies is not compulsory from regulatory 
capital perspective, if the maximum aggregate exposure to one counterparty does 
not exceed the threshold limit of Rs. 7.5 crore, subject to meeting certain other 
conditions.

5.1: Key Fact Statement (KFS) for Retail and MSME Loans & Advances
Applicability All Commercial Banks (including Small Finance Banks, Local Area Banks and Regional Rural 
Banks, excluding Payments Banks),All Primary (Urban) Co-operative Banks, State Co-operative 
Banks and Central Co-operative Banks
All Non-Banking Financial Companies (including Housing Finance Companies)
Explanation Key Facts Statement (KFS) is a statement of key facts of a loan agreement, in simple and easier 
to understand language, provided to the borrower in a standardised format. Key Facts of a 
loan agreement refers to legally significant and deterministic facts between an RE (Regulated 
Entities)/a group of REs and a borrower that provides basic information required to assist the 
borrower in taking an informed financial decision.
KFS was specifically mandated in respect of loans by scheduled commercial banks to  
borrowers; digital lending by REs; and microfinance loans. 
• REs shall provide a KFS to all prospective borrowers to help them take an informed view 
before executing the loan contract, as per the standardised format provided by RBI
• The KFS shall be written in a language understood by such borrowers. Contents of KFS 
shall be explained to the borrower and an acknowledgement shall be obtained that he/
she has understood the same.
• the KFS shall be provided with a unique proposal number and shall have a validity 
period of at least three working days for loans having tenor of seven days or more, and 
a validity period of one working day for loans having tenor of less than seven days. 
• The KFS shall also include a computation sheet of annual percentage rate (APR), and the 
amortisation schedule of the loan over the loan tenor. APR will include all charges which 
are levied by the RE.
• Any fees, charges, etc. which are not mentioned in the KFS, cannot be charged by the REs 
to the borrower at any stage during the term of the loan, without explicit consent of the 
borrower
• The KFS shall also be included as a summary box to be exhibited as part of the loan 
agreement.
REs to provide a KFS, as per a standardized format (all critical information including cost), to 
all prospective borrowers in case of all retail and MSME term loan products . 
• The KFS format is provided in Annexure C.
Source/

5.2: Cluster financing 
Applicability All Scheduled Commercial Banks
Explanation MSME cluster approach: 
The lead bank of a district shall promote ‘credit-linkage’ in all clusters located within the district. 
The initiatives under promotion of the credit-linkage, shall include but not be restricted to the 
following measures:
• Assessing the credit requirements of the MSE units in the clusters and addressing their credit 
needs directly or facilitating their linkage with other banks operating in the area for credit 
proposals.
• Creating awareness among the MSE units in the clusters regarding the importance of formal 
credit linkage through various forums including financial literacy camps.
• Enabling coverage under various skill development initiatives in the district.
• Focused attention and proactive measures to improve financial services in the underbanked 
clusters.
The banks shall ensure that the credit needs of clusters are appropriately included in the exercise of 
preparation of branch/block level plans so that the same can be aggregated by lead banks to form the 
District Credit Plan (DCP) and subsequently by SLBC /UTLBC Convenor banks to prepare the Annual 
Credit Plan (ACP).

5.3: Penal Charges
Applicability All Commercial Banks (including Small Finance Banks, Local Area Banks and Regional Rural Banks, 
excluding Payments Banks)
All Primary (Urban) Co-operative Banks ,All NBFCs (including HFCs) and All India Financial 
Institutions (EXIM Bank, NABARD, NHB, SIDBI and NaBFID)
Explanation REs use penal rates of interest, over and above the applicable interest rates, in case of defaults / 
non-compliance by the borrower with the terms on which credit facilities were sanctioned. On a 
review of the practices followed by REs for charging penal interest/charges on loans, the following 
instructions are issued by RBI.
I. Penalty, if charged, for non-compliance of material terms and conditions of loan contract by the 
borrower shall be treated as ‘penal charges’ and shall not be levied in the form of ‘penal interest’ 
that is added to the rate of interest charged on the advances. There shall be no capitalisation of 
penal charges i.e., no further interest computed on such charges. However, this will not affect 
the normal procedures for compounding of interest in the loan account.
II. The REs shall not introduce any additional component to the rate of interest and ensure 
compliance to these guidelines in both letter and spirit.
III. The REs shall formulate a Board approved policy on penal charges or similar charges on loans.
IV. The quantum of penal charges shall be reasonable and commensurate with the non-compliance 
of material terms and conditions of loan contract without being discriminatory within a 
particular loan / product category.
V. The quantum and reason for penal charges shall be clearly disclosed by REs to the customers 
in the loan agreement and most important terms & conditions / Key Fact Statement (KFS) as 
applicable, in addition to being displayed on REs website under Interest rates and Service 
Charges.
VI. Whenever reminders for non-compliance of material terms and conditions of loan are sent to 
borrowers, the applicable penal charges shall be communicated. Further, any instance of levy 
of penal charges and the reason therefore shall also be communicated.
Source/ 
Reference
RBI Master Direction - Lending to Micro, Small & Medium Enterprises (MSME) Sector dated July 24, 
2017 (updated from time to time)

5.5: Pre-payment Charges on Loans
Applicability All Regulated Entities (REs)
Explanation Availability of easy and affordable financing to Micro and Small Enterprises (MSEs)1 is of 
paramount importance. However, Reserve Bank’s supervisory reviews have indicated divergent 
practices amongst Regulated Entities (REs) with regard to levy of pre-payment charges in case of 
loans sanctioned to MSEs which lead to customer grievances and disputes. Further, certain REs 
have been found to include restrictive clauses in loan contracts/ agreements to deter borrowers 
from switching over to another lender, either for availing lower rates of interest or better terms 
of service. Accordingly, as announced in the Statement on Developmental and Regulatory 
Policies dated October 9, 2024, a draft circular in this regard was issued on February 21, 2025 
for public consultation.
Based on a review of the supervisory findings and public feedback received on the draft circular, 
the Reserve Bank, in exercise of the powers conferred by Sections 21, 35A and 56 of the Banking 
Regulation Act, 1949, Sections 45JA, 45L and 45M of the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934 and 
Section 30A of the National Housing Bank Act, 1987,the following Directions has been issued 
by RBI.
3. (i) These Directions shall be called the Reserve Bank of India (Pre-payment Charges on 
Loans) Directions, 2025.
 (ii) These Directions shall be applicable to all loans and advances sanctioned or renewed 
on or after January 1, 2026.
4. These Directions shall apply to all commercial banks (excluding payments banks), co-
operative banks, NBFCs and All India Financial Institutions.
KNOW YOUR LENDER, GROW YOUR BUSINESS
14
5. An RE shall adhere to the following Directions regarding levy of pre-payment charges on all 
floating rate loans and advances:
 (i) For all loans granted for purposes other than business to individuals, with or without 
co-obligant(s), an RE shall not levy pre-payment charges;
 (ii) For all loans granted for business purpose to individuals and MSEs, with or without co-
obligant(s):
 (a) A commercial bank (excluding Small Finance bank, Regional Rural bank and Local 
Area bank), a Tier 4 Primary (Urban) Co-operative bank, an NBFC-UL, and an All 
India Financial Institution shall not levy any pre-payment charges.
 (b) A Small Finance bank, a Regional Rural bank, a Tier 3 Primary (Urban) Co-operative 
bank, State Cooperative bank, Central Cooperative bank and an NBFC-ML shall  
not levy any pre-payment charges on loans with sanctioned amount/ limit up to 
Rs. 50 lakh.
 (iii) The Directions at paragraphs 5(i) and 5(ii) above shall be applicable irrespective of the 
source of funds used for pre-payment of loans, either in part or in full, and without any 
minimum lock-in period.
 (iv) Applicability of above Directions for dual/ special rate (combination of fixed and 
floating rate) loans will depend on whether the loan is on floating rate at the time of 
pre-payment.
6. An RE shall not levy any charges where pre-payment is effected at the instance of the RE.
7. The applicability or otherwise of pre-payment charges shall be clearly disclosed in the 
sanction letter and loan agreement. Further, in case of loans and advances where Key Facts 
Statement (KFS) is to be provided as specified in the Reserve Bank’s circular dated April 15, 
2024 on ‘Key Facts Statement for Loans and Advances’, the same shall also be mentioned in 
the KFS. No pre-payment charges which have not been disclosed as specified herein shall 
be charged by an RE.
8. An RE shall not levy any charges/ fees retrospectively at the time of pre-payment of loans, 
which were waived off earlier by the RE.
Source/ 
Reference
Reserve Bank of India (Pre-payment Charges on Loans) Directions, 2025

7.1: The Reserve Bank - Integrated Ombudsman Scheme, 2021
Applicability All commercial banks, Non-Banking Financial Companies (NBFCs), Payment System Participants, most 
Primary (Urban) Cooperative Banks, and Credit Information Companies
(i) All Commercial Banks, Regional Rural Banks, Scheduled Primary (Urban) Co-operative Banks and 
Non-Scheduled Primary (Urban) Co-operative Banks with deposits size of Rupees 50 crore and 
above as on the date of the audited balance sheet of the previous financial year,
(ii) All Non-Banking Financial Companies (excluding Housing Finance Companies) which (a) are 
authorised to accept deposits; or (b) have customer interface, with an assets size of Rupees 100 
crore and above as on the date of the audited balance sheet of the previous financial year,
(iii) All System Participants as defined under the Scheme.
(iv) Credit Information Companies
Explanation Reserve Bank of India (RBI) integrated its three erstwhile Ombudsman Schemes viz. (i) the Banking 
Ombudsman Scheme, 2006, (ii) the Ombudsman Scheme for Non-Banking Financial Companies, 2018, 
and (iii) the Ombudsman Scheme for Digital Transactions, 2019, into one Scheme -The Reserve Bank 
- Integrated Ombudsman Scheme, 2021. The Scheme simplifies the grievance redress process at RBI 
by enabling the customers of Regulated Entities (REs) like banks, Non-Banking Financial Companies 
(NBFCs), Payment System Participants (PSPs) and Credit Information Companies to register their 
complaints at one centralised reference point. The objective of the Scheme is to resolve the customer 
grievances involving ‘deficiency in service’ on part of REs in a speedy, cost-effective and satisfactory 
manner.
i. ‘One Nation One Ombudsman’ approach by making the RBI Ombudsman mechanism jurisdiction 
neutral.
 The salient features of the Scheme are:
 a. It will no longer be necessary for a complainant to identify under which scheme he/she 
should file complaint with the Ombudsman.
ii. The Scheme defines ‘deficiency in service’ as the ground for filing a complaint, with a specified 
list of exclusions. Therefore, the complaints would no longer be rejected simply on account of 
“not covered under the grounds listed in the scheme”.
iii. The Scheme has done away with the jurisdiction of each ombudsman office.
iv. A Centralised Receipt and Processing Centre has been set up at RBI, Chandigarh for receipt and 
initial processing of physical and email complaints in any language.
v. The responsibility of representing the Regulated Entity and furnishing information in respect of 
complaints filed by customers against the Regulated Entity would be that of the Principal Nodal 
Officer in the rank of a General Manager in a Public Sector Bank or equivalent.
vi. The Regulated Entity will not have the right to appeal in cases where an Award is issued by the 
ombudsman against it for not furnishing satisfactory and timely information/documents.
vii. The Executive Director-in charge of Consumer Education and Protection Department of RBI would 
be the Appellate Authority under the Scheme.
 a) Complaints can continue to be filed online on https://cms.rbi.org.in.
 b) Complaints can also be filed through the dedicated e-mail or sent in physical mode to the 
‘Centralised Receipt and Processing Centre’ set up at Reserve Bank of India, 4th Floor, Sector 
17, Chandigarh - 160017 in the format.
 c) The contact center with Interactive Voice Response System (IVRS) with Toll Free #14448 is 
available 24x7, while the facility to connect to Contact Centre personnel is available from 8:00 
AM to 10:00 PM (Monday through Saturday, except National Holidays) for English and Hindi, 
and ten Regional Languages (Assamese, Bengali, Gujarati, Kannada, Marathi, Malayalam, 
Odia, Punjabi, Telugu and Tamil).”
 d) The Contact Centre will provide information/clarifications regarding the alternate grievance 
redress mechanism of RBI and to guide complainants in filing of a complaint.
vi. A complaint can be filed through any of the following methods:
 a. Online - on CMS portal of RBI at https://cms.rbi.org.in.
 b. Physical complaint (letter/post) in the form as specified in Annexure ‘A’ in the Scheme to 
“Centralised Receipt and Processing Centre, 4th Floor, Reserve Bank of India, Sector -17, 
Central Vista, Chandigarh - 160017”.
Source/Reference https://cms.rbi.org.in/cms/indexpage.html#eng

8.1: Non Performing Asset (NPA)
Applicability All MSME Borrowers
Explanation A loan is called a Non-Performing Asset (NPA) when the borrower fails to repay the loan—
either the interest or the principal amount for more than 90 days. For regular loans, this 
means  a repayment delay of over 90 days.  In the case of accounts with overdraft or cash 
credit facilities, if the account remains inactive or overdrawn beyond the allowed limit for 
90 days, it is also treated as an NPA.
Source/ 
Reference
Master Circular- Prudential norms on Income Recognition, Asset Classification and 
Provisioning pertaining to Advances
https://rbi.org.in/Scripts/BS_ViewMasCirculardetails.aspx?id=12472
8.2: Impact of NPA on Borrowers
Applicability All MSME Borrowers
Explanation When MSME loan is classified as a NPA, it faces immediate consequences. The credit scores 
of both the enterprise and its promoters drop sharply, making it difficult to secure fresh 
loans or renegotiate existing credit. Future financing often comes at higher interest rates. 
Lenders may initiate legal recovery actions, including seizure of assets. The enterprise 
also becomes ineligible for key government benefits such as CGTMSE-backed loans and 
interest subvention schemes. Operationally, the business may struggle with working capital 
shortages, delayed production, or even shutdown. The NPA tag also damages the MSME’s 
reputation, eroding trust among suppliers, partners, and customers
Source/ 
Reference
Master Circular - Prudential norms on Income Recognition, Asset Classification and 
Provisioning pertaining to Advances
https://rbi.org.in/Scripts/BS_ViewMasCirculardetails.aspx?id=12472
8.3: Impact of NPA on Lenders
Applicability All Lending Agencies
Explanation NPA has a direct and adverse impact on lenders. Provisioning for bad loans directly reduces 
profitability, while erosion of capital affects compliance with Basel capital adequacy 
norms. Mounting NPAs can also lead to credit rating downgrades, raising the bank’s cost of 
borrowing. Operational resources are diverted toward recovery efforts, legal proceedings, 
and account monitoring. This increased risk aversion often translates into restricted credit 
flow, particularly to MSMEs and new borrowers. Ultimately, persistent NPAs undermine 
depositor and investor confidence, weakening trust in the institution.
Source/ 
Reference
Master Circular - Prudential norms on Income Recognition, Asset Classification and 
Provisioning pertaining to Advances
https://rbi.org.in/Scripts/BS_ViewMasCirculardetails.aspx?id=12472

Applicability All Lending Agencies
Explanation 1. Trade Receivables Discounting System (TReDS):
A platform for discounting invoices and bills of MSME, facilitating prompt realization of 
receivables. There are five operational TReDS platforms:
 Receivables Exchange of India Limited 
 M1xchange
 Invoicemart
 KredX 
 C2TReDS 
Key Features 
a. Digital Platform: Entire process—from invoice uploading to payment is done online.
b. Participants Involved: MSME Suppliers, Corporate Buyers (Large companies, PSUs, 
Government departments), Financiers (Banks & NBFCs), Invoice Discounting.
c. Invoice Discounting:  MSME upload their invoices. Once approved by the buyer, 
financiers bid to offer the best discounting rate. The MSME gets payment within 1-3 
days.
d. No Collateral Required: Discounting is done without collateral, purely on the strength 
of the buyer’s creditworthiness.
e.  Transparent Pricing: Competitive bidding among financiers leads to better pricing for 
MSME.
2. PSB Loans in 59 Minutes:
A digital platform for credit facilities, providing  business loans to MSME through a contactless 
and efficient process.
Key Features:
a. Quick Approval: In-principal loan approvals granted within 59 minutes
b. Wide Range of Lenders: Integrated with multiple Public Sector Banks (PSBs), private 
banks, and NBFCs
c. Loan Amount: Credit facilities ranging from Rs. 1 lakh to Rs. 5 crore
d. Digital Integration: End-to-end process—from application to sanction—is digitized
e. Secure & Transparent: Secure login and data protection with real-time status tracking
f. Eligibility Analysis: Automated assessment of borrower profile and matching with 
suitable lenders
g. No Collateral Required: Loans can be availed under the CGTMSE scheme without 
collateral

3. GST Sahay:
GST Sahay Invoice Based Financing Scheme enables GST registered applicants to get offer 
of credit facilities by way of invoice-based financing (IBF) from SIDBI. GST Sahay facilitates 
Working Capital either for purchases (called Purchase Invoice Based Financing-PIBF) or credit 
sales (called Sales Invoice Based Financing-SIBF) based on the requirement of the customers. 
GST Registered, Udyam Registered Micro & Small Enterprises [MSEs] can apply for GST Sahay 
IBF limit.
Key Features:
a. Invoice-Based Financing: Offers collateral-free, short-term working capital loans based 
on GST invoices both for purchases and sales.
b. Fully Digital Process: End-to-end paperless journey using Aadhaar, Unified Payments 
Interface (UPI), e-sign, and eNACH for seamless disbursement.
c. Quick Disbursal: Funds are typically disbursed within 24 hours, addressing urgent 
liquidity needs of MSMEs.
d. Cash Flow-Based Assessment: Creditworthiness is evaluated using real-time GST data, 
ensuring faster and more accurate underwriting.
e. Built on OCEN Framework: Leverages Open Credit Enablement Network (OCEN) and 
Account Aggregator (AA) systems for secure, scalable lending
IV. Jan Samarth:
A national portal launched by the Government of India to provide a single platform for all 
credit-linked government schemes. Beneficiaries can digitally check eligibility in few simple 
steps, apply online under eligible scheme and receive digital approval. 
Key Features:
a. Unified Access to Government Schemes:  Currently there are 7 loan categories under 
each loan category there are various schemes listed
b. Wide Lender Network: Connects users with public and private sector banks and NBFCs.
c. User-Friendly & Multilingual: Offers a simple interface in multiple Indian languages.

Applicability All Lending Agencies
Explanation 1. Pradhan Mantri MUDRA Yojana (PMMY)
The Pradhan Mantri MUDRA Yojana (PMMY) provides MUDRA loan, up to Rs.20 lakh, to 
income generating micro enterprises engaged in manufacturing, trading and services sectors. 
The overdraft amount of Rs. 10,000 sanctioned under PM Jan Dhan Yojana (PMJDY) has been 
also classified as MUDRA loans under PMMY. 
Key Features
The MUDRA loans are extended under following categories and funding needs of the 
beneficiary micro unit / entrepreneur. 
a. Shishu: covering loans up to Rs. 50,000/-
b. Kishor: covering loans above Rs.50,000/- and up to Rs. 5lakh 
c. Tarun: covering loans above Rs. 5 lakh to Rs.10 lakh
d. Tarun Plus: covering loans above Rs.10lakh to Rs. 20lakh (for those entrepreneurs who 
have availed and successfully repaid previous loans under the ‘Tarun’ category).
 • Any individual including women, proprietor, partnership firm, private limited 
company or any other entity are eligible applicant under PMMY loans, whose loan 
requirement is up to Rs. 20 lakhs.
 • The guaranteed coverage of PMMY for loans up to Rs. 20 lakhs will be provided 
under the Credit Guarantee Fund for Micro Units (CGFMU).
2. Prime Minister’s Employment Generation Programme (PMEGP):
PMEGP is a credit-linked subsidy scheme that helps individuals set up new micro-enterprises 
in the manufacturing and service sectors. 
Key Features
a. The maximum project cost is Rs 50 lakh for manufacturing and Rs 20 lakh for services. 
b. Beneficiaries receive a subsidy ranging from 15% to 35%. 
c. No collateral is required for loans up to Rs 10 lakh. 
d. Entrepreneurship Development Programme mandatory training (10 days) for all 
beneficiaries.
e. Online application & monitoring through the PMEGP e-portal.
f. The scheme is implemented by the Khadi and Village Industries Commission (KVIC) 
3. PM Vishwakarma Scheme:
PM Vishwakarma Scheme provides holistic assistance including skill training, modern 
toolkits, and financial support to  traditional artisans and craftspeople
Key Features:
a. Recognition: PM Vishwakarma Certificate and ID card.
b. Skill Training: Basic (5–7 days) and Advanced (15+ days), Rs. 500/day stipend during 
training.
c. Toolkit Incentive: Rs. 15,000 via e-voucher for tool purchase.
d. Credit Support: Collateral-free loans in two tranches.
Section – 10
Government Schemes for credit to MSME
KNOW YOUR LENDER, GROW YOUR BUSINESS
23
e. Digital Enablement: Rs. 1 per digital transaction (up to 100/month).
f. Marketing Support: Branding, e-commerce onboarding, quality certification.
g. Udyam Registration: Formal MSME recognition.
4. National Urban Livelihoods Mission (NULM):
The NULM aims to reduce urban poverty by enabling the urban poor to access gainful self-
employment and skilled wage employment opportunities. 
Key Features:
a. Supports individuals and Self-Help Groups (SHGs) in setting up micro-enterprises 
through financial assistance and interest subsidies. 
b. Individuals can avail loans up to Rs 2 lakh, while SHGs can access up to Rs 10 lakh. 
c. The scheme emphasizes skill development, social mobilization, and support for urban 
street vendors.
d. No collateral is required for loans up to Rs 10 lakh, making it accessible for first-time 
entrepreneurs
5. National Rural Livelihood Mission (NRLM):
NRLM, is a flagship rural poverty alleviation program that promotes self-employment and 
financial inclusion through the formation of women-led SHGs. 
Key features
a. These groups are provided with revolving funds, interest subvention, and access to 
collateral-free loans up to Rs 10 lakh. The mission also supports livelihood diversification, 
skill training, and access to public services.
b. It empowers rural women to become financially independent and build sustainable 
income sources through community institutions.
6. MSE GIFT Scheme (Green Investment and Financing for Transformation)
MSE GIFT Scheme aims to promote the adoption of green technologies and clean energy in 
Micro and Small Enterprises (MSEs), making them more sustainable and climate resilient.
Key Features
a. 2% interest subvention on term loans up to Rs 2 crore.
b. Credit guarantee covering 75% of eligible loans.
c. Promotes green technology adoption in sectors like clean energy, waste management, 
etc.
7. MSE-SPICE Scheme (Scheme for Promotion and Investment in Circular Economy)
Key Features:
a. Credit Linked Capital Subsidy for Circular Economy or waste management projects  - 25% 
of investment in plant & machinery or Rs.12.50 lakh, whichever is lower.
b. For Projects costing up to Rs 50 lakh  the maximum subsidy is Rs. 12.5 lakh.
d. Focus on circular economy sectors (plastic, rubber, e-waste).
e. Encourages compliance with Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR).

Check List of Documents to be submitted by the customer
1. Proof of identity – Voter’s ID Card/Passport/Driving License/PAN Card/signature identification from present bankers 
of proprietor, partner or Director (if a company).
2. Proof of residency – Recent telephone bills, electricity bill property tax receipt/voter’s ID Card of proprietor, partner 
or Director (if a company)
3. Proof of business address
4. Proof of Minority
5. Last three years balance sheets of the units along with income tax/sales tax returns etc. (Applicable for all cases from 
Rs.2 lakhs and above). However, for cases below fund based limits of Rs.25 lakhs if audited balance sheets are not 
available then un-audited balance sheets are also acceptable as per extant instructions of the bank. For cases of Rs.25 
lakhs and above, the audited balance sheets are mandatory.
6. Memorandum and articles of Association of the Company/Partnership Deed of partners etc.
7. Assets and liabilities statement of promoters and guarantors along with latest income tax returns.
8. Rent Agreement (if business premises on rent) and clearance from pollution control board if applicable.
9. SSI registration or any other registration certificate, if applicable.
10. Projected balance sheets for the next two years in case of working capital limits and for the period of the loan in case 
of term loan (For all cases of Rs.2 lakhs and above)
11. In case of takeover of advances, sanction letters of facilities being availed from existing bankers/Financial Institutions 
along-with detailed terms and conditions.
12. Profile of the unit (includes names of promoters, other directors in the company, the activity being undertaken, 
addresses of all offices and plants, shareholding pattern etc. (APPLICABLE FOR CASES WITH EXPSOURE ABOVE RS.25 
LAKHS)
13. Last three years balance sheets of the Associate/Group Companies (If any). (APPLICABLE FOR CASES WITH 
EXPOSURE ABOVE Rs25 LAKHS)
14. Project report (for the proposed project if term funding is required) containing details of the machinery to be acquired, 
from whom to be acquired, price, names of suppliers, financial details like capacity of machines, capacity utilization 
assumed, production, sales, projected profit and loss and balance sheets for the next 7 to 8 years till the propose loan 
is to be paid, the details of labour, staff to be hired, basis of assumption of such financial details etc. (APPLICABLE 
FOR CASES WITH EXPOSURE ABOVE RS. 25 LAKHS)
15. Review of account containing month-wise sales (quantity and value both), production (quantity and value), imported 
raw material (quantity and value), indigenous raw material (quantity and value), value of stocks in process, finished 
goods (quantity and value), debtors, creditors, bank’s outstanding for working capital limits , term loan limits ,bills 
discounted. (APPLICABLE FOR CASES WITH EXPOSURE ABOVE RS, 25 LAKHS.)
16. Photocopies of lease deeds/title deeds of all the properties being offered as primary and collateral securities.
17. Position of accounts from the existing bankers and confirmation about the asset being Standard with them (In case of 
takeover).
18. Manufacturing process if applicable, major profile of executives in the company, any tie-ups, and details about raw 
material used and their suppliers, (details about the buyers, details about major competitors and the company’s 
strength and weakness as compared to their competitors etc. (APPLICABLE FOR CASES WITH EXPSOURE ABOVE RS. 
25 Lakh)
(The Check is list is only indicative and not exhaustive and depending upon the local requirements at different 
places addition could be made as per necessity).

KNOW YOUR LENDER, GROW YOUR BUSINESS
70
Definitions
Amortization Schedule -An amortization schedule outlines a loan’s repayment plan, detailing how each payment 
is divided between principal and interest, and showing the remaining loan balance over time.
Asset Based Lending - Loans are provided against the value of physical assets like inventory, receivables, machinery, 
or property
Base Rate - Minimum interest rate a bank can charge for all loans. Banks are not permitted to resort to any lending 
below Base Rate, except in a few special cases
Bill Discounting- Short-term financing method that helps businesses get immediate cash by using their unpaid 
invoices or bills
Business Loan- Loan provided to entrepreneurs or companies to support various business needs—such as 
expanding operations, purchasing equipment, managing cash flow
Cash Flow Based Lending-Loans are sanctioned based on the borrower’s projected future cash flows and repayment 
capacity.
Collateral Security or Collateral - Security provided in addition to the primary security, in connection with the 
credit facility extended by a lender to a borrower
Collateral-based lending- Collateral-based lending is a form of secured lending where a borrower pledges an 
asset—such as property, equipment, inventory, or financial securities, as security to obtain a loan
Credit Guarantee- A government or institutional assurance to the lender that the loan will be repaid, even if the 
borrower defaults
Creditor- An individual or institution that extends credit or lends funds and is eligible to receive repayment.
Debt- Debt refers to borrowed funds that must be repaid, typically with interest. It represents a financial obligation 
or liability for the borrower.
Debt Service Coverage Ratio (DSCR)- DSCR reflects the borrower’s ability to repay current debt obligations
Debtor- an individual or entity that owes money to a lender or creditor, often as a result of availing a loan or credit 
facility.
 Debt-to-Equity Ratio (D/E)- D/E ratio measures how much debt a business is using compared to its own capital
Equated Monthly Instalment (EMI)-The  fixed amount a borrower pays every month to repay a loan over a specified 
period. Each EMI includes both the interest and a portion of the principal amount.
Equity- Equity refers to the ownership interest held by shareholders in a business entity
Factoring- Short-term financing where a business sells its unpaid invoices (also called receivables) to a third party—
called a factor, to avail immediate cash.
Fixed Rate-Loan interest rate that stays the same throughout the loan period.
KNOW YOUR LENDER, GROW YOUR BUSINESS
71
Floating Rate-Loan interest rate that can changes over time, based on market conditions or benchmark rates set 
by the RBI
Interest rate- Interest rate is the cost of borrowing money, expressed as a percentage of the principal amount
Loan Against Property (LAP)- A loan where you pledge your residential or commercial property as collateral to get 
funds
Loan-to-Value (LTV) ratio- Measure used in secured loans to assess the risk of lending against an asset.
Moratorium Period-A break period during which the borrower is not required to make loan repayments, usually 
offered at the start of the loan
Personal Loan-An unsecured loan for personal expenses like travel, medical emergencies,
Prepayment- Paying off a loan before the scheduled end date, either partially or fully.
Primary security - Asset created out of the credit facility extended to the borrower and / or which are directly 
associated with the business / project of the borrower for which the credit facility has been extended
Priority Sector Lending- Priority sector lending includes only those sectors as part of the priority sector, that 
impact large sections of the population, the weaker sections and the sectors which are employment intensive such 
as agriculture, and Micro and Small enterprises.
Regulated Entities (REs)- Regulated entities (REs) are organizations that are subject to oversight and regulation by 
the Reserve Bank of India
Secured Loan- Loan backed by collateral-like property, gold etc.
Term Loan- Loan given for a fixed period, repaid in regular EMIs.
Unsecured Loan-Loan given without any collateral, usually based on creditworthiness and income.
Working Capital Loan- A short-term loan used to finance everyday business operations

“ Friends, 
There was a time when the role of the bureaucracy was primarily that of a regulator, one 
that controlled the pace of industrialization and entrepreneurship. But the country has 
now moved far beyond that mind-set. Today, we are creating an environment that 
promotes enterprise among citizens and helps them overcome every barrier. Therefore, 
the Civil Service must become an enabler—not merely the keeper of rule books, but an 
active facilitator of growth. Let me give you the example of the MSME sector. As you 
know, the country has launched the Mission Manufacturing initiative. The success of this 
mission largely depends on our MSME sector. Amidst the changes happening globally, 
our MSMEs, start-ups, and young entrepreneurs now have a historic and unprecedented 
opportunity. In such a scenario, it’s crucial that we become more competitive in the 
global supply chain. We must also remember that MSMEs are not just competing with 
small entrepreneurs—they are competing globally. If a small country offers better ease of 
compliance to its industries, then it will be in a better position to compete with start-ups 
from our country. That’s why we must constantly assess where we stand among global 
best practices. If Bharat’s industries aim to create globally best products, then Bharat’s 
bureaucracy must aim to offer the best ease-of-compliance environment in the world.”

Chief Patron-Hindustan Awam Morcha (Secular) 
Member of Parliament (Lok Sabha) 
Gaya (SC), Bihar. 
  Profile 
 
Name : Jitan Ram Manjhi 
Father’s Name : Late Ramjit Ram Manjhi 
Mother’s Name : Late Sukumari Devi (Sukri Devi) 
Date of Birth : 06-Oct-1944 
Marital Status : Married 
Spouse Name : Smt. Shanti Devi 
Profession : Politician and Social Activist 
Party Name : Hindustan Awam Morcha (Secular) 
Education Qualification : B.A. (HONS), Magadh University, Bodhgaya 
Permanent Address : Vill. Mahkar, PO-Sapneri PS- Mahkar, Distt Gaya, Bihar 824233 
Present Address : 28 Akbar Road, New Delhi-110011 
E-mail : jrmpatna@gmail.com, jrm.sansadgaya@sansad.nic.in   
Mobile : +91-9431800705 
 
 
Political Experience 
 
1. 09-06-2024 onwards       : Union Cabinet Minister of Micro, Small & Medium Enterprises 
                                                                     Government of India. 
         : Elected as a Member of Parliament for 18 th Lok Sabha From Gaya 
                                                       Constituency on 04.06.2024.    
2. 2020 to 2024 (9 June 2024)    :  MLA from 227 Imamganj Constituency 
3. 23-11-2020 to 25-11-2020 : Protem Speaker in Bihar Legislative Assembly. 
4. 2015 to 2020 : MLA from 227 Imamganj Constituency 
5. 20-05-2014 to 21-02-2015 : Chief Minister of Bihar. 
6. 2008 to 2014 ( upto 19 May) : Cabinet Minister in Bihar Government. 
7. 2005 to 2008 : MLA in Bihar from 236 Barachatti  Constituency 
 
 
 
 
8. 2000 to 2005 : MLA in Bihar from 253 Bodhgaya  Constituency 
9. 1998 to 2000 : Minister of State in Bihar Government. 
10. 1988 to 1990 : MLA in Bihar from 255 Fatehpur  Constituency 
11. 1985 to 1988 : Minister of State in Bihar Government. 
12. 1984 to 1985 : Minister of State in Bihar Government. 
13. 1983 to 1984 : Deputy Minister in Bihar Government. 
14. 1980-1982 : MLA  in Bihar from 255 Fatehpur Constituency 
 
Significant Achievements 
 
 Launched many schemes for the welfare of the poor. 
 Many important steps were taken to improve the education and health sector. 
 Many important initiatives were taken for social justice and equality in Bihar. 
 Made significant contributions in social and cultural fields. 
 Always active for the welfare of Dalits and backward classes. 
 Worked in the interest of the public in collaboration with various social organizations. 
 Many schemes and projects were launched for the upliftment of the weaker sections of the society. 
 Made significant contributions towards settlement of disputes between Landlords and Landless laborers.  
 
 
Other Important Information 
 
 President of District Gaya Congress Committee from 1990-95; 
 District President of Janta Dal, Gaya District from 1996-97; 
 Member of Syndicate of Magadh University for 6 years;  
 Regional Head of Bihar Mushahar Sewa Sangh; 
 Member of National Executive Committee and General Secretary (at National Level) of 
       Janta Dal (United).; 
 Formation of Hindustani Awaam Morcha( Secular) in June, 2015 and elected as its 
National President from 25th August, 2015; 
 Participated in many social movements and remained associated with many social 
organizations.  
 
Foreign Countries Visited 
 
 England 
 Thailand 
 
Hobbies 
 
 Reading Newspaper 
 Agricultural Work

In	Rural	 Development		and	Panchayath	 Raj,Sushri	Karandlaje	 was	 instrumental	 in	 providing	 basic	 amenities	 to	 rural	 areas	besides	 bringing

Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) for use of Ministry of Micro, Small and Medium 
Enterprises (M/o MSME) logo support 
Ministry of MSME has been, from time to time, receiving requests from Industry 
Associations, Export Promotion Councils, Non-Government Organization (NGOs), Not for 
Profit Organization, etc. for use of Ministry's logo support for events being organized for 
MSME sector by them in India and abroad, without any financial support and legal binding from 
the Ministry. 
2. Although the Ministry is supportive of events that are being organized for the 
development and promotion of the MSME sector and provide a platform for showcasing the 
strengths of this section, including the range of products and services, certain precaution has to 
be exercised before permission for use of Ministry's logo support for such events, as this may 
sometimes result in misuse of Ministry's logo or even create misgivings in the minds of public. 
3. The Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) for use of Ministry's logo support by 
establishments, offices and officers of the M/o MSME, Indian Embassies / Missions abroad, 
Industry Associations, Non-Government Organization (NGOs), Not for Profit Organization etc., 
is as under: 
I. Use of Ministry's logo support will be allowed without any permission for the 
following categories 
(i) All establishments, offices and officers of the M/o MSME, Government of India. 
(ii) The use of Ministry's logo support by Indian Embassies/ Missions abroad will be 
permissible for events, brochures, publicity material and advertisements approved 
under International Cooperation Scheme of this Ministry. 
II. Use of Ministry's logo support may be allowed with the prior approval of the M/o 
MSME, Government of India 
(i) Permission for use of Ministry's logo support for domestic events/seminars/ 
campaigns/conference/fair/exhibition, etc. other than those organized by M/o 
MSME would be considered on case to case basis, keeping in view of the 
following: 
a. Nature and importance of the event; 
b. Profile / track record of the organizers; 
c. Profile of participants and delegates; and 
d. Other deliverables offered by way of exhibition space at the venue, 
distribution of M/o MSME's publicity material in the delegates' kits, etc. 
(ii) All events for which financial support is extended by the M/o MSME, 
Government of India.

III.Use of logo for publications, websites / portals, award, certificate, directory, 
catalogue, letter heads, visiting cards, etc. 
Request for use of Ministry's logo support on publication, websites /portals, award, 
certificate, directory, catalogue, letter heads, visiting cards, etc. would not be 
considered as it may give the wrong impression to the general public about the 
credential of the publications, website/portal, award, certificate, directory, catalogue, 
letter heads, visiting cards, etc. 
IV. Procedure for grant of permission for use of Ministry's logo support 
(i) All requests will be sent to Joint Secretary(SME), M/o MSME, Udyog Bhawan, 
New Delhi - 110011 in the attached format. 
(ii) All requests for use of Ministry's logo support would be examined on the basis of 
above mentioned Standard Operating Procedure(SOP), the merit of the case and 
will be approved by the Joint Secretary(SME), M/o MSME. 
(iii) Requests for use of Ministry's logo support would be considered based on the 
recommendation by the concerned MSME- Development and Facilitation Office 
(MSME-DFO) duly approved by Deputy Director General (DDG)/Additional 
Development Commissioner (ADC), 0/o DC-MSME, M/o MSME. Based on the 
report on credentials/background of the applicant organization provided by the 
concerned MSME-DFO, the request would be submitted for approval of the 
Competent Authority i.e. Joint Secretary(SME), M/o MSME. 
(iv) M/o MSME will be within its right to seek any additional detail in respect of 
request for use of Ministry's logo support before taking any decision. 
(v) All requests must be sent to M/o MSME at least 30 days in advance along with 
complete details as enumerated above. 
(vi) M/o MSME reserves the right to withdraw permission for use of the of Ministry's 
logo support by giving sufficient notice without assigning any reason thereof 
(vii) The right of the M/o MSME to view the proposed design and layout of use of 
Ministry's logo support usage, prior to finalization of the same. 
(viii) Any violation or misuse of Ministry's logo support would liable the concerned 
organizer to be blacklisted for a period of three years from the date of 
communication.

(ii) Proper respect and prominence shall be ensured to the logo of the Ministry and the

Introduction
1.1 Ministry of Micro , Small and Medium Enterprises
1.1.1 Backgr ound: Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises (MSME) sector has emerged as a 
highly vibrant and dynamic sector of the Indian economy contributing around 31.1% of 
India’s GDP , over 48.5% of India’s exports. It contributes significantly to the economy and 
social development of the country by fostering entrepreneurship and generating large 
employment opportunities at comparatively lower capital cost, next only to agriculture. 
MSME are complementary to large industries as ancillary units. This sector contributes 
significantly to inclusive industrial development of the country. An overview of growth and 
performance of MSME sector in the country is provided in Chapter 3.
1.1.2  Ministry of 
MSME envisions a progressive MSME sector by promoting growth and  
development of small business, including khadi, cottage, village and coir industries, in 
cooperation 
with other Ministries/Departments, State Governments and other stakeholders, 
by providing support to existing enterprises, adopting cutting edge technologies and 
encouraging creation of new enterprises. The Ministry is dedicated to fostering inclusive 
growth by efforts which ensure geographic inclusivity of rural and urban areas and 
demographic inclusivity of socially and economically weaker sections such as SC/ST 
communities and promote women entrepreneurship to boost gender parity.
Vision
Sustainable development of globally competitive Micro, Small and Medium 
Enterprises (MSME) as an engine of growth for the Indian economy.

1.1.4 To achie ve the objective of promotion and development of MSME sector in the country, the 
Ministry is supported by a number of statutory and non-statutory bodies under its aegis 
namely, the Office of the Development Commissioner-MSME, the Khadi and Village Industries 
Commission (KVIC), the Coir Board, National Small Industries Corporation (NSIC), National 
Institute for Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises (ni-msme) and Mahatma Gandhi Institute 
for Rural Industrialisation (MGIRI). Details about the mandate and performance of these 
organizations and statutory bodies are provided in Chapter – 4.
1.1.5 The Ministry is al
so committed towards the progressive use of Official Language “Hindi” in 
all its attached Offices, besides, continuous measures on Vigilance, RTI and prevention of 
sexual harassment. Detailed information can be seen at Chapter-7.
1.2 Mandate of Ministry of MSME
1.2.1 On 9 May 2007, the erstwhile Ministry of Small Scale Industries and the Ministry of Agro 
and 
Rural Industries were merged to form the Ministry of MSME, with the mandate in 
respect of all matters of policy and planning relating to and coordination of all measures for 
development of micro, small and medium enterprises, including khadi, cottage, village and 
coir industries. 
1.2.2 Micro
, Small and Medium Enterprises Development Act, 2006 provides a comprehensive 
statutory framework for the recognition, promotion, development, and protection of MSME 
in the country. The key features of the Act include:
 Statutory 
definition and classification of MSME, enabling uniform identification of 
micro, small, and medium enterprises and providing the legal basis for targeted policy 
support.
 Mandatory 
registration framework for MSME, facilitating formal recognition of 
enterprises and enabling their access to government schemes, incentives, and support 
mechanisms.
 Promotion 
and development measures, including provisions for credit facilitation, 
preferential public procurement, creation of dedicated funds, and grant support, 
aimed at enhancing productivity, technology adoption, and market access.
 Legal fr
amework for addressing delayed payments, mandating timely payment to 
micro and small enterprises, providing for penal interest on delayed payments, and 
establishing Micro and Small Enterprises Facilitation Councils (MSEFCs) for dispute 
resolution.
 Strengthened 
financial discipline and transparency, including disclosure of 
outstanding dues to MSME in buyers’ financial statements and disallowance of interest 
on delayed payments as a tax deduction.
 Over
all, MSMED Act, 2006 serves as the cornerstone legislation for MSME governance 
in India, balancing developmental support with legal safeguards to enhance the sector’s 
resilience, competitiveness, and contribution to economic growth.
3
Annual Report | 2025-26
1.2.3 The Khadi and Village Industries Act, 1956: The Khadi and Village Industries Commission 
Act, 1956 provides the statutory framework for the establishment of the Khadi and Village 
Industries Commission for the regulation, and development of the khadi and village 
industries in the country. The Act mandates the Khadi and Village Industries Commission, 
as a statutory body under the Government of India, to plan, promote, facilitate, organize 
and assist in the development of khadi and village industries including measures relating 
to training, raw materials, marketing, research, quality, etc. and other matters connected 
therewith or incidental thereto, in accordance with the provisions of the Act.
1.2.4 Definition of Micro , Small and Medium enterprises:
 To he lp MSME scale operations and access better resources, the investment and turnover 
limits for classification have been increased by 2.5 times and 2 times, respectively. This is 
expected to improve efficiency, technological adoption, and employment generation. 
 Vide Ministry 
of MSME’s Notification dated 21st March, 2025, the criteria for classifying 
enterprises has been revised, w.e.f. 1st April, 2025, as part of the Union Budget 
Announcements 2025-26. The revised classification criteria are as follows:-   
 a micro enterprise, where the investment in plant and machinery or equipment does 
not e
xceed 2.5 crore and turnover does not exceed 10 crore;
 a small enterprise
, where the investment in plant and machinery or equipment does 
not exceed 25 crore and turnover does not exceed 100 crore; and
 a medium enterpr
ise, where the investment in plant and machinery or equipment 
does not exceed 125 crore and turnover does not exceed 500 crore. 
1.2.5	 Government of India supports and supplements the efforts of the State Governments 
and Union Territory Administrations through various initiatives, aimed at encouraging 
entrepreneurship, creating employment and livelihood opportunities, and enhancing the 
competitiveness of MSME in the evolving economic landscape.

Rural Industry (ARI) Division, Administration and Financial Institutions (AFI) Division, 
Administration and Technology Division (A&T), Integrated Finance Wing (IFW) and Data 
Analytics and Technical Co-ordination (DATC) Wing, besides the Office of the Development 
Commissioner (DC, MSME) as an attached office, and other subordinate organisations. The 
organogram of the Ministry is depicted below.

1.7 Grievance Monitoring
 The Ministry addr esses all grievances through the Centralized Public Grievance Redress 
and Monitoring System (CPGRAMS). In addition to CPGRAMS, the Ministry has launched 
the Samadhaan system, an online grievance monitoring platform specifically designed to 
track and manage other grievances and suggestions received from MSME. Complementing 
these mechanisms is the Champions portal, which serves as a holistic platform for redressal, 
guidance, and handholding of MSME, offering support in finance, marketing, technology, 
and capacity building, while also disseminating information in eleven regional languages 
via 69 State control rooms nationwide.
8
Annual Report | 2025-26
1.7.1 MSME Samadhaan:  T o address issues of Delayed Payment to MSE
 Sections 15–24 
of MSMED Act, 2006 provide a statutory mechanism to address delayed 
payments to Micro and Small Enterprises (MSE). Where payments are delayed beyond 45 
days, MSE may seek redressal through Micro and Small Enterprises Facilitation Council 
(MSEFC) constituted in each State/UT .
 To 
further strengthen this framework, Ministry of MSME launched MSME Samadhaan Portal 
in October 2017. The portal enables online filing and tracking of delayed payment cases and 
provides visibility of outstanding dues from CPSEs, Central Ministries, State Governments, 
and other buyers. Complaints filed on the portal are required to be registered by the 
concerned MSEFC within 15 days.
 Sev
eral States, including Punjab, Maharashtra, Telangana, Odisha, Tamil Nadu, Uttar Pradesh, 
West Bengal, and Delhi, have established multiple MSEFCs to ensure timely and effective 
disposal of cases.
 Since inception 
of the Portal, up to 31st December 2025, MSE have filed 2,56,892 applications, 
involving an amount of ₹ 55,244.31 crore. Of these, 24,238 cases have been resolved through 
mutual settlements, totalling ₹ 3,018.37 crore. Additionally, 52,744 applications are yet to 
be reviewed by MSEFC, involving ₹ 8,397.25 crore, while 47,088 applications have been 
converted into cases, involving ₹ 14,243 crore. In total, 53,911 cases have been successfully 
disposed of by MSEFCs, involving an amount of ₹ 14,638.38 crore.
 A special 
sub-portal within the Samadhaan portal, launched on 14th June 2020, enables 
the reporting of dues and monthly payments by GoI Ministries and CPSEs to MSE. All new 
delayed payment cases are being filed on newly launched MSME ODR Portal w.e.f. 15th 
October, 2025, which is being integrated with the Samadhaan Portal.
1.7.2 Champions Portal
 The Champions portal is a platform for resolution, redressal and remedies. It is a facility 
provided by the Ministry of MSME for: -
 Ensuring a speedy
, convenient, and effective redressal of MSME grievances.
 Handholding and 
helping MSME in their navigation of various government schemes/
policies.
 Pro
viding guidance and advisory services in areas of Finance, Marketing, Technology, 
Raw Material, Labour, Infrastructure and Capacity Building.
 Connecting MSME 
with key officials of Ministry, State Governments, Lending 
Institutions and Government agencies.
 Disseminating of information and details of all schemes of the Ministry of MSME.
 During the 
financial year 2025–26, Ministry of MSME CHAMPIONS portal received a total 
of 39,494 grievances, out of which 39,387 were successfully redressed by the CHAMPIONS

Control Room. This reflects a disposal rate of 99.72 percent, reflecting the portal’s strong

 Moreo

1.8 Procurement 
1.8.1 Public Procur ement Policy
 To 
ensure an assured market for Micro and Small Enterprises (MSE), Ministry of MSME 
notified the Public Procurement Policy for MSE under MSMED Act, 2006, effective from 1st 
April 2012 and mandatory w.e.f. 1st April 2015.
 In No
vember, 2018, the Hon'ble Prime Minister launched the "Support and Outreach 
Programme" for MSME sector, which included 12 key initiatives for MSME growth. In 
line with this, the Public Procurement Policy for MSE Order, 2012 was amended on 9th  
November 2018 vide Gazette Notification No. S.O. 5670(E), setting the annual procurement 
target to 25%, including 4% from SC/ST-owned MSE and 3% from women-owned MSE.
1.8.2 MSME Sambandh P
ortal for Public Procurement from MSE
 To 
ensure effective monitoring of procurement by Central Government Ministries, 
Departments, and CPSEs, Ministry of MSME launched MSME Sambandh Portal in December, 
2017. The portal facilitates the implementation of the Public Procurement Policy for MSE by 
tracking procurement data.  As indicated by the portal, 48.27% of the total procurement of 
₹ 1,90,276.67 crore is reported by 180 CPSEs which is done from MSE benefitting 2,11,251 
MSE. Of this, only 3.50% was from women owned enterprises and 1.62% from SC-ST owned 
enterprises.  
1.8.3 Gov
ernment e-Market Place (GeM)
 Ministry of 
MSME is actively encouraging MSME to onboard the GeM (Government 
e-Marketplace) Portal. To simplify this process, a dedicated option has been integrated into 
the Udyam Registration online form, allowing MSME to express their willingness to join 
GeM seamlessly.
 As of 14th January 2026, data from the GeM portal highlights the total number of onboarded 
MSE along with their corresponding order value, reflecting the increasing participation and 
contribution of MSME in public procurement.

1.9 Skill Tr aining Eco-system of Ministry of MSME 
 Ministry of MSME has developed a comprehensive skilling ecosystem to meet the workforce 
needs of MSME across emerging and traditional sectors, focusing on employability, 
entrepreneurship, and wage employment. Skill training is delivered through KVIC, Coir 
Board, NSIC, ni-msme, and MSME Technology Centres, catering to participants from school 
dropouts to advanced graduates.
 Progr
ammes offer certifications, diplomas, and postgraduate qualifications, including 
specialised skill upgradation for Khadi, Village Industries, and the Coir sector. All trainings 
are aligned with the NSQF under the Skill India Mission and implemented through schemes 
such as TR and IT , TCSP , ATI, National SC/ST Hub, Khadi Gramodaya Vikas Yojana, Coir Vikas 
Yojana, and Mahila Coir Yojana, along with customised, industry-driven courses.

1.10.2 Rs 50,000 crore Equity infusion for MSME through Self Reliant India Fund (Fund of 
F
unds)
 The objecti
ve of Self Reliant India Fund (SRI Fund) is to address the challenge of 
providing finance to MSME through equity infusion. It is essential to identify pools 
of capital which can be further leveraged in order to create a multiplier effect on the 
economy and, therefore, making MSME more competitive in the global markets. 
 Under Aatmanir
bhar Bharat Package, the Government of India announced to set up an 
INR 10,000 crore Fund of Funds for MSME. This fund, which is called Self Reliant India 
(SRI) Fund, has the objective of supporting Venture Capital (VC) / Private Equity (PE) 
firms investing in MSME sector.
 This F
und addresses the equity funding challenges of MSME sector and provides a 
thrust to break their barriers, and allow them to grow to their full inherent potential 
to become global champions. SRI Fund is able to channelize diverse variety of funds 
into under-served MSME and address the growth needs of viable and high growth 
MSME. 

14
Annual Report | 2025-26
 In order to implement the SRI Fund, on 1st September, 2021, a Special Purpose 
V
ehicle (SPV) viz; National Small Industry Corporation (NSIC) Venture Capital Fund 
Limited (NVCFL) got registered as an Alternative Investment Fund (AIF) Category-II. 
Subsequently, the Contribution Agreement amongst (i) Ministry of MSME, Government 
of India; (ii) National Small Industries Corporation Limited; (iii) NVCFL; and (iv) SBI-
CAP Ventures Limited was signed on 12th October, 2021.
 The funds 
to be mobilised by the Daughter funds and committed by SRI Fund are in 
the ratio of 4:1. The SRI Fund is likely to create a multiplier effect, providing significant 
liquidity of INR 50,000 cr to MSME sector and thereby enabling economic growth of 
the country.
 Status:  
Since its inception in October 2021, as on 31.12.2025, 69 Daughter Funds have been 
empanelled with NVCFL (Mother Fund) and these daughter funds have assisted 693 MSME 
by way of investment of ₹  16,260 crore in these MSME. Out of 693 MSME assisted, 90 MSME 
are led by Women entrepreneurs, 22 MSME are led by OBC entrepreneurs and 3 MSME are 
led by SC/ST entrepreneurs.

  Special Campaign 5.0, launched by Ministry of MSME on 2 October 2025, focused on 
institutionalizing Swachhata, disposal of e-waste and obsolete scrap, and clearing pendency 
related to PMO/VIP references, public grievances, parliamentary assurances, and file 
reviews.
 Ministry and its 
field formations achieved 100% targets on 7 parameters and over 95% on 
3 others, alongside improvements in workplace cleanliness and efficiency. By 31st October, 
2025, 23,162 physical files were reviewed and 4,280 were weeded out; 953 e-files were

office space.

to measures for institutionalizing cleanliness and reducing pendency, Ministry

Introduction
 During the year, Ministry of MSME organized and participated in several landmark events,

IITF–2025, PM Vishwakarma and National SC–ST Hub Conclave, MSME Seva Parv–2025:

strengthening the MSME ecosystem and advancing the vision of Viksit Bharat.
2.1. Udyam Utsav
 Ministry of MSME successfully organized ‘Udyam Utsav’ at Rashtrapati Bhavan from March 
20th to March 30th, 2025, celebrating India’s MSME sector. The event was inaugurated by 
Hon’ble President and interacted with the artisans and appreciated their contribution.
 The e
vent showcased indigenous crafts, entrepreneurship, and innovation through seven 
pavilions, featuring handicrafts, agro-based products, Green MSME Technologies, Women 
and Tribal Entrepreneurs, and PM Vishwakarma beneficiaries.
 Seventy (70) exhibitors from across the country participated, presenting their products 
and innovations. The event attracted over 1.5 lakh visitors, generating business worth 
₹
77 lakh. Visitors experienced live pottery, trade exhibits, and cultural performances 
like Hunar Sangeet, Nukkad Natak, and Saree Draping Sessions.

 Ministry of MSME observed MSME Day 2025 on 27 June 2025 at Vigyan Bhawan, New Delhi, 
in a hybrid mode, with participation of over 1,300 stakeholders. The event was graced by 
the Hon’ble President of India, Smt. Droupadi Murmu, as the Chief Guest, along with Hon’ble 
Union Minister for MSME, Shri Jitan Ram Manjhi, Hon’ble Minister of State for MSME, Sushri 
Shobha Karandlaje and Chairman KVIC Shri Manoj Kumar. 
Hon’ble President of India Addressing the gathering on MSME Day 2025
 Speaking on the occasion, the Hon’ble President of India underscored that MSME form a strong 
pillar of the Indian economy, contributing significantly to GDP , innovation, and employment 
generation, particularly in rural and backward areas. Hon’ble President emphasized that a 
robust MSME ecosystem is essential for sustainable and inclusive economic development, 
as MSME generate employment at a relatively low cost of capital while empowering weaker 
sections and decentralizing development. The Hon’ble President noted that Government 
initiatives such as revision of MSME classification norms, enhanced credit access, mandatory 
public procurement from MSE, and skill development under PM Vishwakarma Yojana have

2.3. 17th Civil Services Day, 2025
 On the occasion of the  17 th Civil Services Day, Hon’ble Prime Minister Shri Narendra 
Modi addressed the nation’s civil servants on 21 April 2025 at Vigyan Bhawan, New Delhi, 
and conferred the Prime Minister’s Awards for Excellence in Public Administration. The 
Government of India celebrates April 21 every year as ‘Civil Services day’ as an occasion 
for the Civil Servants to rededicate themselves to the cause of citizens and renew their 
commitments to public service and excellence in work. This date is chosen to commemorate 
the day when first Home Minister of Independent India, Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel addressed 
the probationers of Administrative Services Officers in 1947 at Metcalfe House, Delhi. He 
referred to civil servants as the ‘steel frame of India’ .  
  In his address, Hon’ble Prime Minister noted that 
this year’s Civil Services Day is 
particularly significant as the nation celebrates 75 th year of the Constitution and 150 th  
birth anniversary of Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel. Hon’ble Prime Minister underscored that 
as India advances with the resolve of building a Viksit Bharat, responsibility resting 
on civil servants is both immense and historic. Emphasising that “every wheel of the 
chariot of progress must move together,” he called upon officers to remain citizen-
centric, integrity-driven, and innovation-oriented, and to work with unwavering  
determination to ensure holistic development , leaving no village, no family, and no 
citizen behind.

Administration aim to acknowledge outstanding governance practices and innovations by 
district, state, and central government organizations.
 
 Under the Innovation (Central) category, Ministry of MSME was conferred the Prime 
Minister’s Award for Excellence in Public Administration 2024 for the PM Vishwakarma 
Scheme, in recognition of innovative practices adopted during its implementation at the 
Central Government level.

avilion, themed “Vibrant MSME, Viksit Bharat”, showcased the diversity and

 PM V ishwakarma scheme was launched by the Hon’ble Prime Minister on 17.09.2023, to

a conclave provided an interactive platform for aspiring and existing entrepreneurs

2.11. ODR Portal: F eatured in the Economic Survey 2025-26
 A key initiative of the year, the ODR Portal, found mention in the Economic Survey, 2025-26. 
The r
elevant excerpt in provided below:
 “The persistence of delayed payments remains a critical challenge for the MSME 
sector, 
with an estimated ₹8.1 lakh crore locked in delayed payments, impacting working capital 
and restricting growth. When an MSME files a delayed payment case against a buyer, 
it may strain or even damage the business relationship. Buyers may perceive the filing 
as an adversarial step and may stop placing new orders or discontinue the partnership 
altogether. Since MSMEs rely heavily on long-term commercial ties, the fear of losing 
future business prevent them from pursuing legal option, even when large dues remain 
pending.”
 “The MSE 
Scheme on Online Dispute Resolution (ODR) for Delayed Payments, along with 
the MSME ODR Portal developed under it, introduces a structured process that encourages 
amicable settlement between seller and buyer before the dispute moves into formal 
adjudication under the MSMED Act, 2006. This early, dialogue-based resolution mechanism 
allows MSMEs to recover delayed payments without undermining business relationships.”
30
Annual Report | 2025-26
 “The portal is designed for accessibility, featuring an end-to-end digital process that is 
faster and more cost-effective than traditional channels. Its low-cost structure, multilayered 
resolution mechanism (combining negotiation, conciliation, and arbitration), and 24X7 
availability in multiple languages make dispute resolution economically viable even for very 
small claims. Ultimately, the ODR initiative enhances trust, promotes contractual discipline, 
and can directly ease the cash-flow stress that plagues the MSME ecosystem, ensuring 
greater resilience and competitiveness.” 
 Since its launch, the ODR Portal has disposed of 17 cases within 
eight months, with 
15 cases resolved at the Pre-MSEFC (amicable settlement) stage and 2 cases at the 
conciliation stage, resulting in settlement of ₹60.60 lakh.

3.2. Registration of MSME
3.2.1 Udyam Registration Portal: The Udyam Registration Portal serves as the foundational 
3Chapter
32
Annual Report | 2025-26
digital platform for formal recognition of MSME under MSMED Act, 2006, enabling uniform 
identification of enterprises and their seamless access to government schemes, incentives, 
and statutory benefits. Designed as a simple, online and paperless system, Udyam registration 
is free of cost and based on self-declaration, significantly reducing compliance burden and 
promoting ease of doing business. Udyam Portal has replaced the earlier Udyog Aadhaar 
Memorandum (UAM), creating a dynamic and credible MSME database integrated with PAN 
and GST systems. All existing and prospective entrepreneurs can register on the Udyam 
Registration Portal (https://udyamregistration.gov.in).
 Ministry of 
MSME, vides notification no. S.O. 4926 (E) dated 18.10.2022 amended 
Notification No. S.O. No. 2119(E) dated 26.06.2020 to extend the non-tax benefits to MSME. 
The amendment provides that “In case of an upward change in terms of investment in plant 
and machinery or equipment or turnover or both, and consequent re-classification, an 
enterprise shall continue to avail of all non-tax benefits of the category (micro or small or 
medium) it was in before the re-classification, for a period of three years from the date of 
such upward change."
 Ministry of 
MSME, vides notification no. S.O. 4926 (E) dated 18.10.2022 amended 
Notification No. S.O. No. 2119(E) dated 26.06.2020 to extend the non-tax benefits to MSME. 
The amendment provides that “In case of an upward change in terms of investment in plant 
and machinery or equipment or turnover or both, and consequent re-classification, an 
enterprise shall continue to avail of all non-tax benefits of the category (micro or small or 
medium) it was in before the re-classification, for a period of three years from the date of 
such upward change."
3.2.2  Udy
am Assist Platform:   Ministry of MSME, in association with Small Industries 
Development Bank of India (SIDBI), launched a portal Udyam Assist Platform (UAP), for 
bringing Informal Micro Enterprises (IMEs) into the formal ambit, on 11.01.2023. The 
Government has notified that the certificate issued on the UAP to IMEs shall be treated 
at par with Udyam Registration Certificate for the purpose of availing Priority Sector 
Lending (PSL) benefits. This has helped the registered IMEs to avail the benefits of PSL and  
on-board IMEs on the UAP to formalize the enterprises which are exempted from GST 
regime.
 As on 
31.01.2026, a total number of 7,61,12,097 MSME, including IME on UAP are registered. 
There are 1,59,56,949 enterprises registered in Manufacturing category, 2,72,74,600 
enterprises registered in Service sector and 3,28,80,548 enterprises registered in Trading 
category.
 One of 
the critical indicators to assess the successful growth of MSME Sector in an economy 
is the data of registration of new MSME; it also depicts the conducive environment for 
starting such enterprises in an economy as well as the high morale of entrepreneurs.

Introduction 
 Several organizations under Ministry of MSME support policy implementation and  
sect
oral development. Key institutions include the Office of the Development 
Commissioner (MSME), KVIC, Coir Board, NSIC, ni-msme, and MGIRI, which contribute 
to entrepreneurship development, skill upgradation, employment generation, innovation, 
and promotion of rural and traditional industries, thereby supporting inclusive and 
sustainable growth of MSME sector.
4.1  Office of De velopment Commissioner (MSME)
 Office of the Development Commissioner (MSME) is an attached office of the Ministry of 
MSME, headed by the Additional Secretary and Development Commissioner (MSME). Office 
of DC(MSME) assists Ministry of MSME in formulating, coordinating, implementing and 
monitoring various policies and programmes for the promotion and development of MSME 
with a network of field offices located across the country. 
 It has 
a vast network of  field organizations and institutions across the country which 
includes 33 MSME Development and Facilitation Offices (MSME-DFOs), 31 Branch MSME-
DFOs, 2 MSME Development Nucleus Cell (Ladakh and Lakshadweep), 7 MSME Testing 
Centres (MSME-TCs), 7 MSME-Testing Stations (MSME-TSs), 18 Technology Centres (also 
known as Tool Rooms and Technical Institutions (TRs and TIs).

vising the government in policy formulation for the promotion and development of 
MSME.

services to MSME.

infrastructure.

er development as a vehicle for MSME Ecosystem development.

olving and coordinating Policies and Programmes for development of MSME as ancillaries 
to large industries including CPSUs. 
• Enhancing trade competiti
veness to ensure increase in share of export basket.

o credit.
