4.3. COIR BOARD
4.3.1. Introduction
 Coir Board 
is a statutory body established under the Coir Industry Act, 1953 for promoting 
the overall development of coir industry in India. The Head Quarters of the Board is located 
at Coir House, M.G. Road, Kochi, Kerala. The Board is running 45 establishments including 
24 Showrooms and Sales Depots in various parts of the country. There are a total number of 
207 employees under the Board. 
Hon’ble Union Minister for MSME, Shri Jitan Ram Manjhi, during his visit to the Coir Board 
Headquarters, Kochi, on 11th January 2025, reviewing the activities of the Coir Board with senior 
officials of the Ministry and interacting with representatives of coir associations
4.3.2. Objectiv e
 The main 
objective of the Board is promoting the overall development of the coir industry 
and improvement of living conditions of the workers engaged in this traditional industry.
4.3.3. Functions
K
ey functions of the Coir Board include:
 Undertaking, assisting 
or encouraging scientific, technological and economic research and 
maintaining and assisting in the maintenance of one or more research institutes;
 Promoting e
xports of coir yarn and coir products and carrying on propaganda for that 
purpose.
 Regulating 
under the supervision of the Central Government the production of husks, 
coir yarn and coir products by registering coir spindles and looms for manufacturing coir 
products as also manufacturers of coir products, licensing exporters of coir, coir yarn and 
coir products and taking such other appropriate steps as may be prescribed;
 Licensing of 
retting places and warehouses and otherwise regulating the stocking and sales 
of coir fibre, coir yarn and coir products both for the internal market and for exports.
 Ad
vising on all matters relating to the development of the coir industry.
62
Annual Report | 2025-26
4.3.4. Coir Industry in India
  India is the 
largest producer of Coir and Coir Products in the world, accounting for more 
than 70% of the total production. The coir sector in India is very diverse and involves 
households, co-operatives, NGOs, manufacturers and exporters. The coir industry employs 
about 7 lakh people, of whom majority are from rural areas belonging to the economically 
weaker sections of the society. Nearly 80% of the coir workers are women. Originating as 
a traditional agro-based industry in Kerala, the coir sector has expanded to other coconut-
producing states such as Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, Odisha, West Bengal, 
Maharashtra, Assam, and Tripura. With its rich heritage and export-oriented focus, the coir 
industry holds significant potential for growth through value addition and technological 
advancements.

4.5 National Institute for Micr o, Small and Medium Enterprises (ni-msme) 
 ni-msme w as originally set up as Central Industrial Extension Training Institute (CIETI) 
in New Delhi in 1960 under the then Ministry of Industry and Commerce, Government of 
India. The Institute was shifted to Hyderabad in 1962 as a registered Society in the name of 
Small Industry Extension Training Institute (SIET). 
 With the 
enactment of MSMED Act, 2006, the Institute expanded focus of its objectives 
and re-designated its organization structure. In line with the new Act, the Institute was 
restructured as National Institute for Micro, Small and Medium enterprises (ni-msme). 
4.5.1 Objectiv
e
 ni-msme primaril
y functions as a Trainer of Master Trainers. With evolving technology and 
market dynamics, it has expanded its mandate to include consultancy, research, education, 
extension, and information services.
 Aligned with 
national objectives of industrial and economic development, the Institute 
focuses on key areas such as: Innovation and Incubation, Entrepreneurship, Skill 
Development, Technology Upgradation and Transfer, Policy Support, NGO Networking, 
Environmental Sustainability, Cluster Development, Management Consultancy,  
Quality Services, Digital Marketing and Branding, Financial Services, and Information 
Services.

4.6 Mahatma Gandhi Institute for R ural Industrialisation (MGIRI)
4.6.1  Mahatma Gandhi initiated All India Village Industries Association (AIVIA) in 1934 at 
Maganwadi, Wardha Maharashtra to support rural industrialization through Science and 
Technology interventions. In 1955, Jamnalal Bajaj Central Research Institute (JBCRI) was 
established to continue AIVIA’s activities under Khadi and Village Industries Commission 
(KVIC). Between 2001 and 2008, JBCRI was revamped with support from IIT Delhi and KVIC, 
leading to the creation of Mahatma Gandhi Institute for Rural Industrialization (MGIRI). 
MGIRI was set up as a national institute under the Ministry of MSME, at Maganwadi, Wardha 
and began autonomous functioning from 2008.
Hon’ble Union Minister for MSME, Shri Jitan Ram Manjhi Chaired 12 General Council (GC) Meeting of MGIRI, Wardha.
4.6.2 Objectiv e:
The main objectives of the institute as enunciated in its Memorandum of Association include:
 To 
accelerate rural industrialization for sustainable village economy so that KVI sector co-
exists with the main stream
 Attr
act professionals and experts to Gram Swaraj
 Empow
er traditional artisans
 Innov
ation through pilot study/field trials 
 R
esearch and Development for alternative technology using local resources 
4.6.3 Functions:
The activities of MGIRI are being carried out by its six divisions each headed by a Senior Scientist/ 
Technologist. 
i. Rur
al Chemical Industries Division: The main focus of this division is to promote quality 
consciousness and consistency in the area of food processing, organic foods and other 
products of rural chemical industries.  It also provides a comprehensive quality testing 
support and is working towards developing field worthy kits, techniques and technologies 
to facilitate the cottage and small-scale units in this area.
78
Annual Report | 2025-26
ii. Khadi and Textile Division: The activities mainly carried out by this division are to improve 
the productivity, value addition and quality of products manufactured in khadi institutions 
by introducing new technologies and by providing quality assurance support. It also works 
towards facilitating eco-friendly products and methods
iii. Bio-processing 
and Herbal Division: This division of MGIRI prepared technology package 
and simple quality assurance methods to facilitate production and utilization of organic 
manures, bio-fertilizers and bio-pesticides to promote rural entrepreneurs. This section 
is also making efforts to develop new formulations using ‘Panchagavya’ and their quality 
assurance procedures and facilities.
iv. Rur
al Energy and Infrastructure Division: This division has been mandated to develop user-
friendly and cost-effective technologies utilizing commonly available renewable resources of 
energy to facilitate rural industries and also to carry out audit of traditional rural industries 
so as to make them energy efficient.
v. Rur
al Crafts and Engineering Division: This division has been mandated to develop user-
friendly and cost-effective technologies utilizing commonly available renewable resources of 
energy to facilitate rural industries and also to carry out audit of traditional rural industries 
so as to make them energy efficient.
vi.  Management 
and Systems Division: This division provides information and communication 
technology based solutions for rural industries with a view to enhance their global 
competitiveness.

 MGIRI has 
a General Council (GC), President of the GC is the Hon’ble Union Minister of 
MSME, Govt. of India and an Executive Council (EC) lead by the Secretary of the Ministry of 
MSME, Govt. of India as the Chairman. The Director of the Institute is the Member Secretary 
of both GC and EC.

Major Schemes, Programs and  
Initiatives
Introduction 
 Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises (MSME) serve as the backbone of economic 
development, contributing significantly to employment generation, industrial output, and 
exports. Recognizing their pivotal role, the government has introduced a range of initiatives 
to address the diverse needs of this sector. These initiatives span across critical areas, 
including Credit and Financial Assistance, Skill Development Training, Infrastructure 
Development, Marketing Assistance, Technological and Quality Upgradation, and 
Other Services. Together, these schemes aim to empower MSME by fostering innovation, 
improving competitiveness, and ensuring sustainable growth, thereby strengthening their 
contribution to the nation's economy. A detailed list of the Schemes of Ministry of MSME is 
given below.
5.1. Credit and Financial Assistance
 Access to affordable and adequate credit is critical for the growth and sustainability of 
MSME. 
Recognizing this, the government has introduced several schemes aimed at providing 
financial support to MSME, enabling them to meet their operational and expansion needs. 
Below are the major schemes under this theme: 
5.1.1. PM Vish wakarma Scheme
 Launched in September, 2023, with an initial outlay of ₹ 13,000 crore, ‘PM Vishwakarma’ 
aims to enhance the quality and reach of artisans’ products and services, integrating them 
into domestic and global value chains. The scheme provides holistic support to help artisans 
move up the value chain, improving their trades, socio-economic status, and quality of life.
Objective:
 To 
enable the recognition of artisans and craftspeople as Vishwakarma under 18 trades, 
making them eligible to avail all the benefits under the Scheme. 
 To 
provide skill upgradation to hone their skills and make relevant and suitable training 
opportunities available to them.
5Chapter
82
Annual Report | 2025-26
 To provide support for better and modern tools to enhance their capability, productivity, 
and quality of pr
oducts and services. 
 To 
provide the beneficiaries an easy access to collateral free credit and reduce the cost of 
credit by providing interest subvention.
 To 
provide incentives for digital transactions to encourage digital empowerment of 
Vishwakarmas. 
 To 
provide a platform for brand promotion and market linkages to help them access new 
opportunities for growth.
Key Highlights:
The Scheme provides of the following benefits to the artisans and crafts persons:
 Rec
ognition: Recognition of artisans and craftspeople through PM Vishwakarma certificate 
and ID card.
 Skill Upgr
adation: Basic Training of 5-7 days and Advanced Training of 15 days or more, 
with a stipend of ₹ 500 per day;
 Toolkit 
Incentive: A toolkit incentive of upto ₹ 15,000 in the form of e-vouchers at the 
beginning of Basic Skill Training.
 Credit 
Support: Collateral free ‘Enterprise Development Loans’ of upto ₹ 3 lakh in two 
tranches of ₹ 1 lakh and ₹ 2 lakh with repayment tenures of 18 months and 30 months, 
respectively, at a concessional rate of interest fixed at 5%, with Government of India 
subvention to the extent of 8%. Beneficiaries who have completed Basic Training will be 
eligible to avail the first tranche of credit support of upto ₹ 1 lakh. The second loan tranche 
will be available to beneficiaries who have availed the 1st tranche and maintained a standard 
loan account and have adopted digital transactions in their business or have undergone 
Advanced Training.
 Incenti
ve for Digital Transaction: An amount of Re. 1 per digital transaction, upto 
maximum 100 transactions monthly will be credited to the beneficiary’s account for each 
digital pay-out or receipt.
 Mark
eting Support: Marketing support will be provided to the artisans and craftspeople 
in the form of quality certification, branding, onboarding on e-commerce platforms such as 
GeM, advertising, publicity and other marketing activities to improve linkage to value chain.
 The beneficiaries will be regist
ered on Udyam Assist Platform as enterprises.

5.1.2. Prime Minister ’s Employment Generation Programme (PMEGP)
 PMEGP is a central sector scheme being administered by Ministry of MSME which aims at 
generating self-employment opportunities through establishment of micro-enterprises in 
84
Annual Report | 2025-26
non-farm sector for rural as well as urban areas. PMEGP is in operation since 2008-09 and 
has been approved for continuation over the 15th Finance Commission cycle i.e., for the 
period of five years from FY 2021-22 to FY 2025-26. The Scheme is being implemented by 
Khadi and Village Industries Commission (KVIC), as the nodal agency at the National level. 
At the State level, the scheme is implemented through State offices of KVIC, State Khadi and 
Village Industries Boards (KVIBs), District Industries Centres (DICs), Coir Board (for coir 
related activities) and Banks.
Key Objectives:
 The scheme 
aims to generate employment opportunities in rural as well as urban areas of the 
country through setting up of new self-employment ventures/projects/micro enterprises. 
 The scheme 
seeks to provide continuous and sustainable employment to all segments of 
traditional and prospective artisans and rural/urban unemployed youth in the country, 
thereby curbing the migration of youth from rural to urban areas.
Highlights:
 Margin 
Money (subsidy) for setting up new micro-enterprises would be provided at the 
rate of 25% of project cost for rural areas and 15% for urban areas in the case of General 
category and 35% for rural areas and 25% for urban areas for Special category.
 The maximum 
cost of the project/ unit admissible under manufacturing sector is Rs.50 lakh 
and under business/service sector is ₹20 Lakh.
 All new 
units setup under PMEGP will be mandatorily registered under Udyam Portal before 
Physical Verification of the Unit and the adjustment of the Margin Money in the PMEGP 
beneficiary loan account.
 Geo-tagging of the PMEGP units ha
ve been initiated for capturing the details of the products 
and services offered by the units and to create market linkages for them.
 PMEGP applicatio
ns were being accepted from beneficiaries in physical form in 11 regional 
languages since January 2024 excluding Hindi and English. However, since June 2025, offline 
applications are being accepted in 19 regional languages i.e., Gujarati, Malayalam, Punjabi, 
Manipuri, Bangla, Marathi, Tamil, Assamese, Kannada, Oriya, Telugu, Bodo, Dogri, Kashmiri, 
Konkani, Nepali, Sindhi, Urdu, Maithili.
Intended Beneficiaries:
 Any indi
vidual, above 18 years of age is eligible under the scheme.
 Prospecti
ve entrepreneurs belonging to Aspirational Districts and Transgender Category 
have been included in the Special Category for higher subsidy.
2nd Loan For Up-Gradation of the Existing PMEGP / REGP / MUDRA Units

5.1.3. Raising and Ac celerating MSME Performance (RAMP) Scheme
 RAMP is a World Bank supported Central Sector Scheme aimed at improving access of 
MSME to market, finance and technology upgradation by enhancing the outreach of existing 
MoMSME schemes.  The programme also aims at strengthening institutions at the Central 
and State level, and enhancing Centre- State collaboration. 
 The total 
project cost of RAMP , for the five years FY 2022-23 to 2026-27 is ₹6062.45Cr, 
of which, World Bank support is ₹3750 Cr (USD 500 million and the rest is Govt of India 
funding.  
Major Objectives:
 Acceler
ating Centre- State collaboration in MSME promotion and development
 Enhancing effecti
veness of existing M/o MSME schemes by technology upgradation
 Strengthening R
eceivable Financing Market for MSME
86
Annual Report | 2025-26
 Enhancing eff ectiveness of Credit Guarantee Trust for Micro and Small Enterprises (CGTMSE), 
and promoting guarantee for greening initiatives of MSE, and women owned MSE
 Reducing incidence of dela
yed payments to MSE 
Key Highlights:
 RAMP scheme 
would enhance the performance of MSME by promoting technology 
upgradation, innovation, digitization, market access, credit, etc through active participation 
of the Central and State Government Departments and Agencies.  
 RAMP scheme 
envisages to benefit more than 5.5 lakh MSME during the programme period 
(FY 2022-23 to 2026-27).  The programme implementation will be through the State 
agencies.
 Under the aegis of RAMP progr
amme, the Ministry has launched four sub-schemes 
i. MSE Gr
een Investments and Financing for Transformation (MSE-GIFT): Provides 
interest subvention and guarantee for selected green technologies. 
ii. MSE Scheme 
for Promotion Investment in Circular Economy (MSE-SPICE): Provides 
capital subsidy to existing MSE for implementing Circular Economy solutions.
iii. MSE-ODR (Online 
Dispute Resolution) for speedy and efficacious resolution of delayed 
payment cases through MSME ODR Portal with an outlay of ₹ 189 cr.
iv. MSME TE
AM   scheme : Provide support for e-commerce enablement of MSME by 
conducting onboarding workshops, providing product cataloguing and handholding/
account management support.
 National MSME Council
• A National 
MSME Council has been constituted under the Chairmanship of the Hon’ble 
Minister, MSME, with Secretaries of allied Central Ministries/ Departments, Principal 
Secretaries of States/UTs as Members. 
• The functions 
of the Council include overseeing inter Central Ministerial / Departmental 
coordination, Centre-State synergies and progress on the reforms mandated in MSME sector 
including RAMP .

5.1.4. Credit Guarantee Fund Trust For MSE (CGTMSE) – 
 Ministry of MSME implements Credit Guarantee Scheme (CGS) for Micro and Small 
Enterprises through Credit Guarantee Fund Trust for Mico and Small Enterprises (CGTMSE). 
Under the Scheme, credit guarantee is provided for extending collateral free and third-
party guarantee free loans to Micro and Small Enterprises (MSE) through Member Lending 
Institutions.
Key highlights:
 The ceiling for guar
antee coverage is ₹ 10 crore (w.e.f.  01.04.2025) per borrower.
 A nominal 
amount towards an Annual Guarantee Fee (AGF) for the credit facility sanctioned 
is charged on the outstanding loan amount.
 The e
xtent of guarantee ranges from 75% to 90% depending upon the quantum of loan and 
type of beneficiary.
 Concession in 
guarantee fee by 10% in respect of loans given to special categories of the 
borrowers (SC/ST owned MSE, MSE owned by Women, MSE owned by Person with Disability, 
MSE owned by Agniveers, MSE owned by Transgenders, ZED certified MSE, MSE situated 
in Aspirational Districts and MSE located in North East Region (including Sikkim), UT of 
Jammu and Kashmir and UT of Ladakh). 
 Recognizing 
the importance of women entrepreneurs, the guarantee coverage for women-
led enterprises has been enhanced to 90% to promote their participation in the economic 
growth of the country.  
Achievements:
 Since its 
inception in FY 2001 till 31.12.2025, a total of 1.35 crore credit guarantees 
amounting to ₹ 12.39 lakh crore have been extended to MSE. 
o 29.67 lakh 
guarantees amounting to ₹ 1.93 lakh crore have been extended to MSE 
owned by Women Entrepreneurs.
o 6.53 lakh 
guarantees amounting to ₹ 22,525 crore have been extended to MSE owned 
by SC Entrepreneurs.
o 2.23 lakh 
guarantees amounting to ₹ 10,713 crore have been extended to MSE owned 
by ST Entrepreneurs.
o 23.72 lakh 
guarantees amounting to ₹ 1.21 lakh crore have been extended to MSE 
owned by OBC Entrepreneurs.
o 40,987 guar
antees amounting to ₹ 3,053 crore have been extended to MSE owned by 
Person with Disability.
 The details 
of the Scheme along with latest circulars are available in the website of CGTMSE 
at  www.cgtmse.in
89
Annual Report | 2025-26
 Since its inception, the CGTMSE Scheme has benefited over 1 crore enterprises, with 1.01 
crore proposals approved for guarantee cover totaling ₹8.07 lakh crore as of December 31, 
2024.
 Beneficiaries of 
the scheme have experienced notable improvements in their turnover and 
employment generation following CGTMSE funding. This has led to a positive impact in 
several key areas of the MSE sector, including technology upgradation, skill development,

 The scheme 
has seen significant geographical expansion, with a strong emphasis on reaching

 More 
than 100 industrial sectors have benefited from the scheme, highlighting its broad

is not confined to major industrial hubs alone; the scheme has also extended its

TMSE has proven to be highly effective in settling claims.

velopment Programme (MDP) – Minimum One Week, 20-25 participants,

vance MDP- Newly added in the guidelines approved for the 15th FC which costs ₹

5.2.3. Assistance t o Training Institution (ATI) scheme
 Assistance is provided to training institutions under the Ministry viz., ni-msme, KVIC, Coir

skill development training programmes. Assistance is also provided to existing State level

ATI scheme.

5.3.1. Micro 
and Small enterprises Cluster Development Programme (MSE-
CDP)
 Ministry of MSME is implementing Micro and Small Enterprises - Cluster Development 
Programme (MSE-CDP) for development of clusters. MSE-CDP is a demand driven Central 
Sector Scheme wherein, State Governments send proposals as per common needs of existing 
cluster in their States/UTs.
Objective:
 To 
enhance the productivity and competitiveness of Micro and Small Enterprises (MSE) 
by extending financial assistance as Government of India (GoI) grant for establishment of 
Common Facility Centers (CFCs) in the existing clusters.
 For 
establishment of new / upgradation of existing Industrial Areas / Estates / Flatted 
Factory Complex. 
93
Annual Report | 2025-26
Key Components of the Scheme:
a. Common F
acility Centers (CFCs): 
 The GoI gr
ant will be restricted to 70% of project cost (₹ 5.00 crore - 10.00 crore) and 60% 
of project cost (₹ 10.00 crore - 30.00 crore).
 In case 
of NER and Hill States, Island territories, Aspirational Districts, LWE affected districts 
and clusters with more than 50% micro/village or women owned or SC/ST owned units, 
GoI grant will be 80% of project cost (₹ 5.00 crore - 10.00 crore) and 70% of project cost (₹ 
10.00 crore - 30.00 crore).
 CFC 
projects with cost more than ₹ 30.00 crore are also considered however, the GoI 
assistance would be on maximum eligible project cost of ₹30.00 crore.
b. Infrastructur
e Development:
 The GoI 
grant will be restricted to 60% of project cost (₹ 5.00 crore - 15.00 crore) for setting 
up of new Industrial Estate / Flatted Factory Complex and GoI grant will be 50% of project 
cost (₹ 5.00 crore - 10.00 crore) for up-gradation of existing Industrial Estate / Flatted 
Factory Complex.
 In case 
of NE and Hill States, Island territories, Aspirational Districts, LWE affected districts 
and clusters with more than 50% micro/village or women owned or SC/ST owned units, 
GoI grant will be 70% of project cost (₹ 5.00 crore - 15.00 crore) for setting up of new 
Industrial Estate / Flatted Factory Complex and 60% of project cost (₹ 5.00 crore - 10.00

 The ID 
project with cost more than ₹ 10.00 crore/15.00 crore are also considered however 
the GoI assistance would be on maximum eligible project cost of ₹ 10.00 crore/15.00 crore.

5.5.1. MSME Champions Scheme 
 MSME Champion s scheme is a holistic approach to unify, synergize, and converge various 
schemes and interventions with a single purpose. The Scheme has been formulated through 
Standing Finance Committee (SFC)) for a period of 5 years i.e., 2021-22 to 2025-26. There 
are 3 components under MSME Champions scheme, the details of which are as below:
 A. MSME Sustainable (ZED) Certification Scheme
 B. MSME-Competitiv
e (Lean) Scheme
 C. MSME-Innov
ative (for Incubation, IPR, Design and Digital MSME) Scheme
Objective:
• To 
select clusters and enterprises and modernize their processes, reduce wastages, sharpen 
business competitiveness, and facilitate their National and Global reach and excellence.
i. MSME Sustainable (ZED) Certification S cheme: 
 Launched in April, 2022, MSME Sustainable (ZED) Certification is a transformative initiative 
aimed at promoting Zero Defect Zero Effect (ZED) practices among MSME.
Key Highlights:
 Aw
areness: Promotes Zero Defect Zero Effect (ZED) practices among MSME.
 Encour
agement for MSME: Inspire MSME to become MSME Champions through the ZED 
Certification journey.
 Benefits of 
ZED Certification: Substantial reduction in wastage, increased productivity and 
operational efficiency, enhanced environmental consciousness and energy conservation, 
optimal utilization of natural resources and expansion of market opportunities.
 Adoption 
of Best Practices: Motivate MSME to standardize products, processes, and 
system.
 Global C
ompetitiveness: Focuses on improving competitiveness through assessment, 
handholding, managerial and technological interventions.
 Sustainability: Encourages 
sustainable practices for long-term growth and environmental 
stewardship.

Annual Report | 2025-26

 Cost of

on ZED Certification cost: Joining reward of ₹ 10,000/- (effectively making bronze

iii. MSME-Innov
ative (for Incubation, IPR, Design and Digital MSME) Scheme
 MSME Inno
vative is an initiative for MSME designed to integrate innovation in incubation, 
design intervention and IPR protection in a single mode approach. 
Objective: 
 It aims 
to raise awareness among MSME about India’s innovation potential and inspire them 
to become MSME Champions. Serving as a hub for innovation, the initiative facilitates and 
guides the transformation of ideas into viable business propositions. The scheme has 3 sub-
components as described below.
a. MSME-Innov
ative (Incubation)
 The scheme 
aims to promote untapped creativity and encourage the adoption of advanced 
technologies in manufacturing and knowledge-based MSME. The intended beneficiaries 
of the scheme are Udyam-registered MSME and other entities. The scheme is being  
implemented through the offices of the Development Commissioner (MSME) under the 
Ministry of MSME. As of 5th Dec 25, an expenditure of ₹28.15 crore has been incurred under 
the Scheme.
Nature of Assistance: 
 Financial assistance 
of up to ₹ 15 lakh per idea is provided to Host Institutes (HIs) for 
developing and nurturing innovative ideas.

oved: Total of 773 HIs across India.

: 52,369 ideas received under Idea Hackathon 5.0, launched by the Hon’ble 
Minister of MSME on 27th June, 2025. HIs have forwarded 13,106 ideas. After documents 
scrutiny, 11088 ideas have been forwarded to Domain Experts Selection Committee (DESC) 
for further evaluation.
b. MSME-Innov
ative (Design)
 The objectiv
e of this component is to create a common platform for collaboration between 
Manufacturing sector and Design expertise/ Design fraternity. It aims to provide expert 
advice and cost-effective solutions for addressing real-time design challenges, supporting 
new product development, its continuous improvement, and value addition to existing or 
new products.

 The intended beneficiaries of the scheme are Udyam-registered Manufacturing MSME.

M/o MSME UNIDO, GEF-5 Projects for MSME across the country
I. Ministry of MSME UNIDO, GEF-5 Project on ‘Promoting Market Transformation for Energy 
Efficiency in Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises’
Description The UNIDO, GEF-5 Project titled 'Promoting Market Transformation for Energy 
Efficiency in MSME aims to foster a supportive market environment for MSME 
by implementing energy-efficient technologies and increasing the adoption of 
these identified technologies within various clusters.
Objective The project aims to enhance energy efficiency within MSME sector by 
establishing and maintaining a revolving fund mechanism to replicate energy 
efficiency measures. 
Energy Target The project targets annual energy savings of 956,184 GJ, amounting to 9,561,838 
GJ over ten years. Additionally, it aims to cut 86,000 tonnes of CO₂ emissions 
annually.
Achievement/ 
Status
• The pr
oject has identified twelve clusters, completing 840 surveys, 89 
energy audits, and 84 baseline energy studies. Over 130 Local Service 
Providers (LSPs) selected to implement approved energy-efficient 
technologies and more than 100 pieces of equipment deployed in 
MSME units. Additionally, 131 expressions of interest gathered from 
clusters for the project's replication phase.
• The w
ebsite launched to share project activities 38 energy-efficient 
technologies identified and approved by the Project Steering Committee 
for demonstration projects, with 38 Excel-based Quick Estimator Tools 
(QETs) developed and toolkits were also prepared. 
• The fr
amework for scaling up demonstrated technologies has been 
finalized through innovative financing.
• Accor
ding to the EMRF structure, financing will be provided by MSME 
development bank SIDBI, while technical services will be delivered by 
EESL. 
Intended 
Beneficiaries
The program targets 12 clusters across seven sectors: Pulp and Paper, Textile, 
Food Processing, Pharmaceuticals, Chemicals and Dyes, Foundry and Forging, 
and Iron and Steel, along with a Mixed Cluster.
Implementation The project is part of the Global Environmental Facilities’ (GEF) programmatic 
framework for energy efficiency in India, with the United Nations Industrial 
Development Organization (UNIDO) serving as the Implementing Agency (IA) 
and Ministry of MSME as the Lead Executing Agency (EA). Energy Efficiency 
Services Ltd (EESL) is the primary executing partner, while the Small Industrial 
Development Bank of India (SIDBI) and the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) 
act as guiding agencies for the project.
100
Annual Report | 2025-26

Description The project aims to implement Concentrating Solar Thermal (CST) technology, 
which captures solar radiation and converts it into heat for use in industrial 
processes, providing heating or cooling.
Objective The project aims to boost the adoption of CST technology for heating and 
cooling in five or more clusters within the GEF-5 funded UNIDO project for 
energy efficiency in MSME clusters.

technology.

e organized across the identified clusters.

ainer Training Programme (TOT) was conducted on June 2024.

oject Steering Committee approved the inclusion of new

facilities were also approved.

Beneficiaries

Implementation United Nations Industrial Development Organization (UNIDO), serving as the

rates with the Ministry of MSME, which functions as the executing agency. The 
GEF-UNIDO project is designed to complement the Ministry of MSME' support

5.5.2. National Scheduled Cast e and Scheduled Tribe Hub (NSSH)
 National Scheduled Caste and Scheduled Tribe Hub (NSSH) was launched by Hon’ble Prime 
Minister in October 2016 which is aimed at promoting entrepreneurship among the SC/
ST and to fulfil the 4% procurement mandate by the CPSEs as laid down in the Central 
Government Public Procurement Policy and to boost entrepreneurship among SC/STs. The 
Hub has undertaken several initiatives to facilitate entrepreneurship among SC/ST MSE  
and to cater the challenge of credit, market linkages, finance facilitation, bid participation, 
etc. 
101
Annual Report | 2025-26
 The Scheme is being implemented through National Small Industries Corporation (NSIC), a 
PSU under this Ministry. Further, to provide mentoring and handholding support to SC/ST 
entrepreneurs across their business lifecycle and tender related participation with CPSEs, 
15 National SC-ST Hub Offices (NSSHOs) have been set up across the country at Ludhiana, 
Agra, Lucknow, Mumbai, Pune, Sindhudurg, Ranchi, Chennai, Bengaluru, Bhubaneswar, 
Guwahati, Kolkata, Surat, Hyderabad, and Bodh Gaya.
 Under the 
Special Credit Linked Capital Subsidy Scheme (SCLCSS) component, SC/ST 
MSE of manufacturing as well as service sectors are eligible for 25% subsidy (upto ₹ 25 
Iakh) for procurement of Plant and Machinery and equipment through institutional credit. 
Further, various skill and entrepreneurship development training programs are being 
provided to SC-ST entrepreneurs across the country through different training institutions. 
These training programs may bridge the existing skill gap, increase the technical as well as 
managerial capabilities of the entrepreneurs and develop entrepreneurship among target  
beneficiaries.
 More 
than 1.59 lakh SC-ST entrepreneurs facilitated across the country since its inception 
under various activities of the scheme.  The scheme has made a positive impact among 
target beneficiaries by way of professional support in upgrading their skills, facilitating 
market linkages and handholding support which is evident from 37-fold increase (in terms 
of value) in public procurement from SC/ST owned MSE i.e., ₹99.37 crore (0.07%) in 2015-
16 to ₹ 3,731.50 Cr. (1.73%) in 2024-25. In FY 2025-26 (as on 14.01.2026), the share of SC/
ST MSE in public procurement is 1.90%.  
5.6.  Digital Gov ernance Initiatives
5.6.1. Inclusiv
e Digital Governance and Multilingual Access
 Ministry of MSME is advancing technology-driven digital governance to deliver equitable, 
transparent, and efficient services to MSME ecosystem in alignment with “Minimum 
Government, Maximum Governance” . These efforts address linguistic, geographic, and 
infrastructural barriers, enabling a citizen-centric, secure, and data-led transformation in 
service delivery, policy dissemination, and grievance redressal mechanisms. 
 A major 
initiative under this framework is integration of the web applications of Ministry of 
MSME with the Digital India Bhashini Programme (MeitY), enabling multilingual access to 
information in 22 Scheduled Indian languages through real-time translation supported by 
domain-specific glossaries. 
 This functionality 
has been implemented across major MSME digital platforms, including 
Ministry of MSME Website, Office of DC MSME Portal, Udyam Registration Portal, Champions 
Portal, and the Zero Defect Zero Effect (ZED) Portal, significantly enhancing accessibility, 
inclusivity, and user experience for entrepreneurs nationwide.
102
Annual Report | 2025-26
 
Multi-lingual Access Enabled on Ministry’s Champions Portal 
 Expansion of multilingual capabilities to additional MSME portals is in progress, further 
strengthening nationwide digital outreach.
Multi-lingual Access Enabled on Ministry’s Udyam Registration and ZED Portal
5.6.2. Data-Driv en Monitoring, Planning and Digital Infrastructure Management
 To strengthen transparency, monitoring, and planning, the Ministry has deployed integrated 
digital 
systems that support data-driven governance and infrastructure oversight. The 
Performance Smartboard serves as a centralized Key Performance Indicators (KPI) 
dashboard, covering thematic areas, such as, Market Support, Credit and Finance Facilitation, 
Technology Infrastructure and Enablement, Entrepreneurship Development, Grievance 
Redressal Mechanisms, Major Schemes, and Udyam Portal insights, using API-based data 
integration and thematic clustering to promote transparency and evidence-based review. 
103
Annual Report | 2025-26
 
Performance Smartboard - Ministry of MSME
 In alignment with the PM GatiShakti National Master Plan (PMGS-NMP), the Ministry has 
complet
ed geo-mapping of nearly one lakh MSME assets across 8 GIS thematic layers and 
44 sub-layers, including ZED-certified MSME and 93,823 PMEGP beneficiaries, supported 
by three-tier validation, live dashboards, and periodic updates.  
 The int
ernal IT Inventory Management Portal, provides centralized oversight of MSME 
web applications, incorporating a centralized application registry, hosting visibility, 
security audit tracking, SSL certificate monitoring with renewal alerts, real-time server and 
resource monitoring through APM tools, system health analysis, automated risk alerts, and 
data integration tracking, collectively enhancing governance, compliance, reliability, and 
cybersecurity of digital services. In addition, the IT and Planning Division of the Ministry 
regularly monitors and reviews e-Office and VPN accounts and undertakes necessary 
actions for deletion or transfer to prevent unauthorized or unnecessary usage.
5.6.3. Cyber Security Strengthening and Compliance
 The Ministry has strengthened  cyber security throug h awareness, monitoring, and clear 
guidelines for MSME. During National Cyber Security Awareness Month 2025 (“Cyber Jagrit 
Bharat”), an awareness session was conducted in collaboration with CERT-In on the 15 key 
cybersecurity elements.
 Another kno
wledge session was conducted under the Chairmanship of Secretary, MSME 
on “Building Cyber Resilience Towards a Cyber Jagrit Bharat” in hybrid mode, as part of the 
National Cyber Security Awareness Month. The knowledge session covered various aspects 
relating to emerging digital threats, financial frauds and redressal mechanisms. Over 1,500 
stakeholders participated in the hybrid session comprising officials of the Ministry, its 
104
Annual Report | 2025-26
organisations, field offices, MSME Associations, and MSME. The key guest speakers included 
senior officials from the Intelligence Fusion and Strategic Operations (IFSO) Special Cell, 
Delhi Police, HDFC Bank, and Punjab National Bank.
 A phishing 
simulation exercise with NIC, alongside sustained awareness messages through 
email, banners, kiosks, and digital channels. In 2025, thirty cyber incidents were reported 
to CERT-In with an average response time of 4.2 hours, and forty-one malware threats were 
quarantined through endpoint detection and response systems. 
 The Ministry 
is implementing CERT-In’s 15 Elemental Cyber Defence Controls to improve 
asset management, system configuration, access control, and incident response.
 A C
yber Security Citizen’s Charter has been prepared and posted on the Ministry’s website 
to enable MSME to stay cybersafe in simple, easy-to-follow language.

6.1.2. “Promotion of MSME in N.E. Region and Sikkim” Scheme
 Introduction: Th e scheme was launched in the year 2016. It was further continued for 
implementation during 15th Finance Commission (2021-2026) with budget outlay of 
₹295.00 Cr.
 The scheme 
provides financial support to create or upgrade infrastructure and common 
facilities for MSME in the NER to supplement manufacturing, testing, packaging, product 
and process innovations and skilling activities.
6Chapter

Annual Report | 2025-26
Scheme Components:  
1. Setting up of ne
w and modernization of existing Mini Technology Centres:
 Objectiv
e:  The Scheme envisages financial assistance to State Governments for setting up 
of  new and modernization of existing Tool Room/ Mini Technology Centers. Projects for 
creation of common facilities to supplement manufacturing, testing, packaging, Research 
and Development, product and process innovations and training for natural resources such 
as fruits, spices, agri, forestry, sericulture and bamboo etc. available in NER and Sikkim are 
given priority. The Geo tagging of the project is mandatory.
 Financial Assista
nce:  The maximum assistance from Government of India will be limited 
to ₹13.50 Cr. or 90% of the project cost, whichever is less. Balance and any excess amount

up-gradation of infrastructure would be limited to ₹1.00 Cr. within the total permissible GoI

cost of land.

elopment of new and existing Industrial Estates:

ve: Government of India financial assistance will be provided for development of 
new and existing Industrial Estates, Flatted Factory Complexes. The infrastructure facilities 
will include power distribution system, water, telecommunication, drainage and pollution 
control facilities, roads, storage and marketing outlets etc. Geo tagging of the project is 
mandatory.

maximum assistance from Government of India will be limited 
to ₹13.50 Cr. for development of New Industrial Estate or ₹ 9.00 Cr. for development of 
Existing Industrial Estate or 90% of the project cost, whichever, is less. Balance and any 
excess amount to be contributed by State Government.

elopment of Tourism Sector:

e:  Looking into immense scope of tourism sector in NER and Sikkim, projects for 
creation of common services such as kitchen, bakery, laundry and dry cleaning, refrigeration 
and cold storage, IT infra, potable water, display centre for local products, centre for cultural

projects with local MSE. Projects of State Tourism Development Agencies or Central/State 
Autonomous bodies for Tourism Development shall be allowed.

b.  The validation of DPR b
y Ministry of Tourism;

d.  Beneficiaries should essentially be link
ed to any renowned e-commerce platform.

6.1.4.1. MSME Mark eting Conclave in Guwahati
 A MSME 
Marketing Conclave was organized by NSIC on 30 October 2025 at ASFEDC 
Auditorium, Guwahati, Assam, under the Trade Enablement and Marketing (TEAM) initiative 
of MoMSME as part of the RAMP program. The event was graced by Hon’ble Minister of State 
for MSME, Sushri Shobha Karandlaje, as the Chief Guest. The conclave focused on the TEAM 
initiative, which aims to digitally onboard 5 lakh micro and small enterprises, particularly 
women entrepreneurs, over the next two years to drive digital transformation.

6.1.4.2. Visit of Hon’ble Minister of State for MSME, to NSIC Guwahati Training Centre
 Hon’ble 
Minister of State for MSME, Sushri Shobha Karandlaje, visited NSIC Guwahati 
Training Centre on 30 October 2025. During the visit, Hon’ble MoS appreciated the skilling 
and entrepreneurship initiatives of NSIC and expressed satisfaction at the large proportion 
of women trainees. Certificates were distributed to successful trainees, and they were 
encouraged to leverage various government schemes upon completion of their training. She 
commended the NSIC Training Centre for its continued efforts in fostering micro-enterprise 
creation.

have been assisted under PMEGP .

6.2.2  The primary focus of the schemes and programmes undertaken by the organizations of 
the Ministry is to provide and facilitate a broad range of services essential for accelerating

the development of MSME. However, there are certain schemes and programmes that are

website at www.msme.gov.in.
6.3. Welfare for Specially Abled Persons
6.3.1    The Ministry maintains a 'reservation roster' in accordance with the government’s guidelines 
to ensure representation of specially abled persons. Vacancies arising from the 100-point 
roster for disabled individuals within the Ministry and its attached offices of DC (MSME), are 
regularly communicated to the Department of Personnel and Training for filling these posts. 
Additionally, various facilities, such as conveyance allowances, are provided to disabled 
employees working in different offices of the Ministry, in line with the Government of India’s 
instructions.
6.3.2   Ministry of MSME's organizations, such as NSIC and ni-msme, are providing necessary 
reservation and preference for training in various fields of entrepreneurship development 
and related training modules. These initiatives are aimed at supporting the growth and 
empowerment of specially-abled individuals.
6.3.3   PMEGP – Under the Prime Minister’s Employment Generation Programme (PMEGP), 
differently-abled persons are categorized as Special Category beneficiaries, entitled to 
a higher rate of subsidy and a lower own contribution. Since its inception (i.e., 2008-09 
to 31st December 2025), a total of 6,005 projects have been assisted for differently-abled 
persons under PMEGP .

6.4. International Cooperation
6.4.1. International C
ooperation Scheme 
 International Cooperation (IC) Scheme is a Central Sector Scheme which aims to build 
capacity 
of MSME for entering export market by facilitating their participation in international 
exhibitions and fairs abroad. 
 Under the 
IC scheme, financial assistance is provided on reimbursement basis to the eligible 
113
Annual Report | 2025-26
Central/State Government organizations/Industry Associations to facilitate participation of 
MSME in the international exhibitions held abroad, organizing international conference in 
India as well as reimbursement of various costs involved in export of goods and services 
with the aim of technology upgradation, modernization, joint venture etc. 
 Under the 
Market Development Assistance component of the Scheme, a total of 293 MSME 
have been facilitated by way of participation in 22 International Fairs/Exhibitions during the 
year 2025. Further, 5,252 MSME have benefitted directly by participating in 18 International 
conferences organised in India.  
 Under the 
Capacity Building of First Time Exporters (CBFTE) component of the Scheme, 
during the year 2025, a total of 19 Micro and Small Enterprises have been assisted by way 
of reimbursement of RCMC (Registration-cum-membership certificate) Charges.
6.4.2. Multilater al and Bilateral matters:
 To str engthen international cooperation within MSME sector, the Ministry has entered 
into  Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) and Joint Action Plans with Tunisia, Romania, 
Rwanda, Mexico, Uzbekistan, Lesotho, Sri Lanka, Algeria, Sudan, Côte d'Ivoire, Egypt, South 
Korea, Mozambique, Botswana, Indonesia, Vietnam, Sweden, UAE, USA and Mauritius.

General Statutory Obligations
7.1 Official language
7.1.1 Government policy envisages the progressive increase in the use of Hindi in official work. 
During 
the year, Ministry of MSME took effective measures to ensure compliance with the 
Official Language Policy, implementation of the Annual Programme, and adherence to 
various orders of the Government of India issued on the recommendations of the Committee 
of Parliament on Official Language.
7.1.2 The Ministry 
of MSME is implementing the Official Language Act, 1963 and the Official 
Language Rules, 1976 (as amended from time to time) to achieve the targets set by the 
Department of Official Language. The Ministry continues to advance towards making Hindi 
the language of government work and meeting the expectations of the Parliamentary 
Committee on Official Language.
7.1.3 During the 
year, in compliance with Section 3(3) of the Official Language Act, 1963, 
documents related to the Parliamentary Standing Committee, Cabinet Notes, notifications, 
resolutions, contracts, agreements, tender forms and notices, rules, parliamentary questions 
and answers, papers laid on both Houses of Parliament, administrative reports, etc. were 
issued in both Hindi and English. All advertisements are released bilingually. The Ministry’s 
website is primarily in Hindi and efforts are on to keep it updated bilingually. Rule 5 of the 
Official Language Rules is fully complied with and all letters received in Hindi are replied to 
in Hindi only.
7.1.4 Monitoring 
of offices under the administrative control of the Ministry was reinforced 
to review progress in Hindi usage and the Ministry team inspected the State Office of 
Khadi and Village Industries Commission, Bengaluru; Zonal Office of National Small 
Industries Corporation Ltd., Bengaluru; ni-msme, Hyderabad and NSIC Headquarters, 
New Delhi. Suggestions were provided to achieve annual programme targets set by the  
Department of Official Language, resulting in visible progress in Hindi work across 
subordinate offices.
7.1.5 Furthermor
e, the Parliamentary Committee on Official Language inspected subordinate 
offices of the Ministry including the Central Coir Technology Institute, Bengaluru; MSME 
Development Office, Bengaluru; KVIC State Office, Dehradun; NSIC Ltd., Noida; MSME 
Development Office Branch, Gwalior; and the Institute for Design of Electrical Measuring 
Instruments, Mumbai. The Committee expressed satisfaction with Hindi progress in almost

all the inspections. In these inspections, the Ministry was represented by the Deputy Director 
General (In charge, Official Language) and other nominated officers. 
7.1.6 As per 
the guidelines of the Department of Official Language and the expectations of the 
Parliamentary Committee on Official Language, the Hindi Salahkar Samiti under the 
chairmanship of the Hon’ble Minister MSME has been constituted.
7.1.7 Hindi Di
was: With a view to promoting the progressive use of Hindi in official work, 
September 2025 was observed as Hindi Month in the Ministry. During the period, eleven 
Hindi competitions were organised, witnessing record participation from officers and staff 
members across various levels.
7.1.8 Sev
eral new and innovative competitions were introduced, including Recitation, Picture 
Analysis, Storytelling, Paragraph Writing and Hindi Grammar, Official Language Knowledge, 
and Vocabulary competitions. These initiatives were aimed at breaking the monotony of 
conventional formats and providing participants with a sense of novelty, thereby encouraging 
enthusiastic and sustained engagement.
7.1.9 The competitions 
were carefully structured to assess participants comprehensively across 
multiple dimensions of Hindi language proficiency. For instance, the Recitation Competition 
focused on pure pronunciation and articulation, featuring running matter on diverse 
themes, including prose passages, poems, and ghazals. Ministry of MSME is reportedly the 
only Ministry to have organised such a specialised competition during Hindi Month.
7.1.10 In line 
with the Digital India initiative, prize money for the winners was transferred online, 
ensuring transparency and efficiency.
7.1.11 Hindi F
ortnight/Week/Month was also celebrated with equal enthusiasm in subordinate 
offices under the Ministry. Notably, for the first time, a joint Hindi Day message from the 
Hon’ble Minister, MSME and the Hon’ble Minister of State was circulated to all subordinate 
offices and was also displayed on the Ministry’s website, further reinforcing the commitment 
to the promotion of Hindi in official communication.

7.2 Vigilance
7.2.1  The Vigilance set up in the Ministry of MSME is headed by a Joint Secretary level officer, who 
acts as part-time Chief Vigilance Officer (CVO) of the Ministry. CVO also acts as the nodal 
point for all vigilance matters, in consultation with the Central Vigilance Commission and 
Investigating Agencies.
7.2.2  The Ministry 
is implementing the guidelines / instructions issued by the Department of 
Personnel and Training and the Central Vigilance Commission in creating greater awareness 
of vigilance among the stakeholders.  During the period under report, the references/ 
vigilance complaints received from various sources in the Ministry / Attached office / 
Organizations under the Ministry were scrutinised / disposed of, in consultation with 
Central Vigilance Commission, wherever applicable. 
7.2.3 The V
igilance Division of Ministry deals with the Vigilance complaints and appeals preferred 
thereon in the cases of Ministry of MSME. It also provides vigilance comments on appeals 
119
Annual Report | 2025-26
preferred by employees of the organisations functioning under the Ministry on the penalties 
imposed on them.  Key functions performed by the Vigilance Division : 
i. Maintenance 
of Annual Performance Appraisal Reports (APARs) of Officers and Staff 
including online system of SPARROW.
ii. All cases 
falling under CCS (Conduct) Rules, 1964 including Annual Property Return 
Statements of the employees.
iii. Vigilance clearance f
or administrative purposes. 
iv. Communication wit
h CVC and seeking its advice in the disciplinary cases of Ministry 
of MSME.
7.2.4 There 
are separate Vigilance Divisions in organisations under the administrative control of 
Ministry of MSME. Vigilance Divisions in these organisations are headed by considerably 
higher-level officers to assist the respective Heads of the organisations.
7.2.5 Annual Sect
oral Review Meeting on Vigilance: The first Annual Sectoral Review Meeting 
with MSME sector was held on 30.12.2025 under the chairmanship of the Chief Vigilance 
Commissioner, Central Vigilance Commission (CVC). The meeting focused on preventive 
vigilance, systemic reforms, and good governance. The importance of MSME sector to the 
economy, along with key initiatives relating to digitalisation and adoption of technology, 
including implementation of the PM Vishwakarma Scheme, was highlighted by the Ministry. 
The CVO, Ministry of MSME, presented the prevailing vigilance scenario and ongoing 
initiatives, followed by organisation-wise presentations by the CVOs.
7.2.6 The meeting 
resulted in actionable measures to strengthen the vigilance framework, 
including expediting disciplinary proceedings, strengthening vigilance inspections, timely 
submission of Quarterly Progress Reports (QPRs), capacity building through training 
programmes, rotation of officials in sensitive posts, improved handling of PIDPI complaints, 
and clarity on the applicability of the Public Procurement Policy (MSE Order, 2012) to 
autonomous bodies.

7.4 Citizen’s Chart er
7.4.1 Citizens’/ Clients’ Charter for M/o MSME have been prepared and the same can be viewed 
at Ministry’s website. This Charter consists of a Declaration of the Ministry, incorporating its 
mission and commitment to MSME and for the people of India, in general.
7.4.2 Annual R
eports, MSME Patrika, Handbook on Self Employment and Know Your Lender 
booklet have been published and are available for the information of potential entrepreneurs, 
policy makers and others. The website of the Ministry, i.e., www.msme.gov.in provides all 
the relevant information and link to its organisations.
7.4.3 The detailed 
Citizens’/ Clients’ Charter of the Ministry is available on the website of the 
Ministry.
7.4.4 Complaints: The 
Department of Administrative Reforms and Public Grievances (DARPG) 
have created a portal for public grievances- http://pgportal.gov.in. Any person can lodge 
his grievance on this portal. All grievances received by DARPG, Prime Minister’s Office 
and President Secretariat are forwarded to the concerned Ministries through this portal/
software. Grievance pertaining to other Ministries/Subordinate organisations can be 
transferred online. Ministry of MSME, Office of DC (MSME), NSIC and other subordinate 
organisations have been provided link to http://pgportal.gov.in. In FY 2025-26 a total of 
3,65,68 grievances were received on the portal and 3,57,06 (97%) were redressed with 
average disposal time of 9 days. 
7.4.5 Ministry of 
MSME has a dedicated Grievance redressal portal CHAMPIONS. A portal for 
addressing grievances related to MSME: www.champions.gov.in.  During the financial year 
2025–26, Ministry of MSME CHAMPIONS portal received a total of 39,494 grievances, 
out of which 39,387 were successfully redressed by the CHAMPIONS Control Room. This 
reflects a disposal rate of 99.72 percent, reflecting the portal’s strong responsiveness and 
the effectiveness of its grievance-r
edressal mechanisms.
7.4.6 Moreo
ver, of the grievances replied during FY 2025-26, 25,503 grievances (around 64.75 
percent) were redressed within 48 hours, demonstrating the portal’s commitment to timely 
support and handholding MSME. 
7.4.7 Recent 
initiatives include periodic reviews of Ministry of MSME grievance redressal 
mechanisms and associated portals, chaired by the Hon’ble Minister of State for MSME. 
These periodic assessments aim to strengthen system responsiveness, improve qualitative 
aspect of grievance, and ensure that MSME receive timely and effective support.

3). MSME COMPETITIVE (LEAN) SCHEME 45
• Credit linked subsidy program for setting up new 
micro-enterprise in non-farm sector.
• Margin Money subsidy ranges from 15% to 35% of project 
cost for projects up to Rs. 50 Lakh in Manufacturing sector 
and Rs. 20 Lakh in the Service sector.
MSME SCHEMES 03  
Prime Minister’s Employment 
Generation Programme (PMEGP)
 
Objective: 
To generate sustainable and continuous employment 
opportunities for rural and unemployed youth as well as 
prospective traditional artisans and thereby halt 
occupational migration.
•
• For beneficiaries belonging to Special categories such as 
SC/ST/ Women/ Minorities/ Ex-Servicemen/ Transgenders/ 
Aspirational districts/NER, the margin money subsidy is 
35% in rural areas and 25% in urban areas.
• The own contribution of the beneficiary is 10% of the project 
cost in case of General category and 5% of the project cost 
in case of Special category (SC/ST/OBC/PH/Women/Ex 
Servicemen/Transgenders/Aspirational Districts/NER) 
beneficiaries.
     
Scheme applicable for: 
• Any individual, above 18 years of age can apply.
Detailed Information: 
For more information and regular updates, visit: www.msme.gov.in
The scheme aims to provide financial assistance to set up 
self-employment ventures and generate sustainable 
employment opportunities in rural as well as urban areas.
•
  MSME SCHEMES 04For more information and regular updates, visit: www.msme.gov.in
• If the application for loan is approved, Banks sanction and 
release the balance amount of 90 to 95% of the total 
project cost suitably for setting up of the units by the 
beneficiaries.
• In order to have sustainability of the projects/units set up 
under the scheme, support services are also provided in 
the form of Backward & Forward Linkages by organizing 
events like workshops, EDP training to the beneficiaries, 
exhibitions, etc.
• Government of India has introduced online process for 
flow of applications and disbursement of Margin Money 
directly to financing branches.
• Online application form is mandatory for individuals on 
the e-portal. The application form/PMEGP MIS portal is 
mobile friendly. SMS/e-mail alerts are sent to the 
applicants automatically by the system or by the 
concerned officials at each stage.
• To increase the registration of MSMEs in the country, the 
Government has undertaken measures for PMEGP units to 
adopt the Udyog Aadhar Memorandum (UAM)/UDYAM 
Registration.
• Model Projects Reports of different activities have been put 
up on PMEGP e-portal for the benefit of potential 
beneficiaries.
  
How to apply: 
• Apply on:  
https://www.kviconline.gov.in/pmegpeportal/pmegphome
 

MSME SCHEMES 05  For more information and regular updates, visit: www.msme.gov.in
1.1)  2nd Loan for up-gradation of the existing 
PMEGP/REGP/ MUDRA units
Objective: 
• With an objective to assist existing units for expansion 
and upgradation, the scheme provides financial 
assistance to successful/well performing units.
• The scheme also caters to the need of the entrepreneurs 
for bringing new technology/ automation so as to 
modernize the existing unit.
• Maximum subsidy would be 15% of the project cost (20% 
for NER and Hill States). The balance amount of the total 
project cost is provided by Banks as term loan.
• Further financial assistance scheme for expansion/ 
upgrade the existing PMEGP/REGP/MUDRA units for 
manufacturing and Service/ Trading units from the year 
2018-19.
• The maximum cost of the project under manufacturing 
sector for up-gradation is Rs.1.00 crore and Rs. 25.00 lakhs 
under Service/ Trading sector.
• Maximum subsidy would be 15% of the project cost (20%) 
for NER and Hill States) i.e. Rs. 15.00 lakhs in Non-NER and 
Rs. 20.00 Lakh for NER and Hill States. The balance amount 
of the total project cost shall be provided by Banks as 
term loan.
     
Scheme applicable for: 
• Existing well performing PMEGP/REGP/MUDRA units.
Detailed Information: 
• All existing units financed under PMEGP/MUDRA Scheme 
whose margin money claim has been adjusted and the 
first loan availed has been repaid in stipulated time are 
eligible to avail the benefits.
• The units should have been making profit for the last 
three years.
• Beneficiary can apply to the same financing bank, which 
sanctioned the loan for their unit, or to any other financing 
bank, which is willing to extend credit facility for second 
loan.
• The beneficiary can choose any implementing agency 
and that may be different from the agency chosen for 1st 
loan.
• The 2nd loan should lead to additional employment 
generation.
• To submit the application under 2nd loan for 
up-gradation, the beneficiaries have to apply by filling 
application form on PMEGP e-Portal.
• Registration of Udyog Aadhar Memorandum 
(UAM)/UDYAM REGISTRATION is mandatory.
MSME SCHEMES 06  For more information and regular updates, visit: www.msme.gov.in  
How to apply: 
• Apply on:  
https://www.kviconline.gov.in/pmegpeportal/pmegphome

Credit Guarantee Scheme for Micro 
& Small Enterprises(CGTMSE)
Objective: 
• To encourage first generation entrepreneurs to venture 
into self-employment opportunities by facilitating credit 
guarantee support for collateral free / third-party guar-
antee-free loans to the Micro and Small Enterprises 
(MSEs).
• Credit guarantee for loans up to Rs. 5 crore, without 
collateral and third-party guarantee.
• Guarantee coverage ranges from 75% to 90%.
• Any collateral/ third-party guarantee free credit facility 
extended by eligible Member Lending Institutions (MLIs) 
to new as well as existing MSEs, including Service 
Enterprises, Trading (Retail/Wholesale Trade) & 
Educational/Training Institutions, with a maximum 
guarantee ceiling of Rs. 5 crore, is eligible for guarantee 
under the Scheme.
     
Scheme applicable for: 
• MSEs registered on Udyam Portal.
Detailed Information: 
Eligible Activity:
• All activities eligible as per MSMED Act.

Micro & Small Enterprises Cluster 
Development Programme(MSE-CDP)  Scheme
Objective: 
• To support the sustainability and growth of MSEs by 
addressing common issues such as improvement of 
technology, skills & amp; quality, market access, etc.
• To create/upgrade infrastructural facilities in the new/ 
existing Industrial Areas/Clusters of MSEs.
• To set up Common Facility Centres (for testing, training, 
raw material depot, effluent treatment, complementing 
production processes, etc).
• Promotion of green & sustainable manufacturing 
technology for the clusters.
• Creation of Common Facility Centers including Plug & 
Play Facilities.
• Support for Infrastructure Development Projects including 
Flatted Factory Complexes.
• COMMON FACILITY CENTRES: Creation of, “Tangible Assets” 
such as Common Production / Processing Centre, Design 
Centers, Testing Facilities including Plug & Play Facilities. 
GoI Assistance: up to 80% of the maximum Project cost of 
Rs. 30 crores.

Entrepreneurship and Skill Development 
Programme(ESDP)
Objective: 
• To promote new enterprises, capacity building of existing 
MSMEs and inculcating entrepreneurial culture in the 
country. 
• Widen the base of entrepreneurship by development, 
achievement, motivation and entrepreneurial skill to the 
different sections of the society.
     
Scheme applicable for: 
• Aspiring and Existing Entrepreneurs.
• Entrepreneurship Awareness Programme (EAP)-One Day 
training programme is conducted for Entrepreneurship/  
Self-employment awareness and motivation to different 
sections of the society including SC/ ST/ Women, differently 
abled, Ex-servicemen and BPL persons as career options. 
• Entrepreneurship cum Skill Development 
Programme(E-SDP) - Six weeks training programme is 
conducted for Entrepreneurship & Skill Training in Agro 
Based Products, Hosiery, Food & Fruit Processing Industries, 
Carpet Weaving, Mechanical Engineering Workshop/ 
Machine Shop, Heat Treatment, Electroplating, 
Basic/Advance Welding/ Fabrication/Sheet metal work, 
Basic/ Advance Carpentry, Glass & Ceramics etc. 
• Advance E-SDP: The One Week Advance ESDP programmes 
are conducted through IIMs/IITs/ICAR/CSIR/BARC/IISC/NIT/ 
Agricultural University of Central and State government etc.

Assistance to Training Institutions 
(ATI) Scheme
Description :
Nature of Assistance :
•
•
The assistance is provided to National level training 
institutions operating under the Ministry of MSME, namely, 
NI-MSME, KVIC, Coir Board, Tool Rooms, NSIC & MGIRI in the 
form of capital grant for the purpose of creation and 
strengthening of infrastructure and support for 
entrepreneurship development and skill development 
training programmes. Assistance is also provided to 
existing State level EDIs i.e. owned and controlled by a State 
Government/UT for creation or strengthening/ expansion of 
their training infrastructure.
Amount of assistance will not exceed the actual amount 
for strengthening/ expansion of the infrastructure required 
by the training institutions of this Ministry. Scale of 
maximum assistance to the State level EDIs will be 
restricted to Rs.3.00 crore in each case. Assistance for skill 
development programmes will be provided as per the 
prescribed rates.
     
Who can apply:  
• Institutions of Ministry of MSME and existing State level EDIs.
Deputy Secretary (EDI), M/o MSME

Coir Vikas Yojana- Umbrella 
Scheme 
Objective: 
I. SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
• To enhance utilization of the raw material abundantly 
available in the country at economic levels of production.
Coir Vikas Yojana (CVY) is an Umbrella Scheme being implemented by 
Coir Board for the Development of Coir Industry all over the country.
• To increase income/returns to workers, entrepreneurs, 
exporters and other stake-holders of the industry.
• Full utilization of the market potential of the products 
within the country and abroad and Industry related 
functional support services.
• Development of improved equipment machinery, 
processes and new products.
• Promoting large scale investment in Coir Industry.
• Development of skilled manpower for Coir industry, 
empowerment of rural women and Employment 
Generation.
• Welfare measures for coir workers.
• To upgrade the coir industry through technological 
interventions and integration of various Digital Platforms.
Under this Umbrella Scheme, the Coir Board is 
implementing the following sub schemes/ programmes:
The Scheme component envisages undertaking various 
Research and Development activities for the 
modernization and technological upgradation of the coir 
industry and extension of the fruit of the research at the 
laboratory level for application at the field level and 
extension of testing and service facility.
MSME SCHEMES 16  For more information and regular updates, visit: www.msme.gov.in
    
 
Under this programme, the Board is imparting stipendiary 
training on value addition processes by providing the 
services of the trainers and with a view to disseminate 
information on the schemes and latest technologies 
available in coir sector and also to attract prospective 
entrepreneurs to this sector, the Board also organizes 
Entrepreneurship Development Programmes, Workshops, 
Seminars, Awareness Programmes, Exposure Tour etc. 
under this Scheme.
a. Skill Upgradation 
Mahila Coir Yojana is a women oriented, self employment 
scheme being implemented by the Coir Board. The 
scheme is intended to provide training with stipend 
facilities and creation of self-employment opportunities to 
rural women artisans in regions producing coconut.
The activities of the Board under Export Market Promotion 
include participation in international exhibitions, publicity, 
providing assistance under Export Market Development 
Assistance Scheme/International Co-operation Scheme 
etc.
The main activities under the Domestic Market Promotion 
scheme are display cum sales of coir products through its 
Showrooms & Sales Depots, participation in domestic 
exhibitions organized by other Agencies, organizing 
exclusive fairs, disbursement of Market Development 
Assistance (MDA) to the Coir Co-operative units, societies 
etc through the State Governments/ UTs concerned.
The activities under this programme would involve primary 
and secondary level information search, collection and 
statistical analysis of information including export, drawing 
inferences and preparation and publication of the reports.
b. Mahila Coir Yojana

International Cooperation (IC) 
Scheme
Objective: 
• The scheme aims to build Capacity of MSMEs for entering 
export market by facilitating their participation in 
international exhibitions/ fairs/conferences/ seminar/ 
buyer-seller meets abroad as well as providing them with 
actionable market-intelligence and reimbursement of 
various costs involved in export of goods and services. The 
Scheme provides opportunities to MSMEs to continuously 
update themselves to meet the challenges emerging
out of changes in technology, changes in demand, 
emergence of new markets, etc. 
As on date, Sub-Component–I and Sub-Component-II are 
in operation. The guidelines of these two components have 
already been circulated which are available on Ministry’s 
website. Sub-Component-III will be in operation soon.
• Sub-Component-I: Market Development Assistance of
MSMEs (MDA).
• Sub-Component-II: Capacity Building of First Time MSE
Exporters (CBFTE).
• Sub-Component-III: Framework for International Market
Intelligence Dissemination (IMID).
     
Eligible Organisations under Sub-Component-I :
• Ministry of MSME and organizations under the Ministry.
• State/Central Gov mernment Organizations/Institutions 
and Registered Industry/Enterprise Associations etc.

Promotion of MSMEs in - 
NER & Sikkim
Objective: 
• The scheme envisaged for providing financial support 
mainly for augmenting Infrastructure development for 
enhancing the productivity, sustainability, competitiveness 
and growth by addressing common issues such as 
improvement of technology, skills & quality, market access 
etc. of Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs).
• Creation of common facilities  to supplement 
manufacturing, testing, packaging, R&D, product and 
process innovations and training for natural resources 
such as fruits, spices, agri, forestry, sericulture and 
bamboo etc. available in NER and Sikkim.
• Development of tourism sector in NER and Sikkim by 
creation of common services such as kitchen, bakery, 
laundry & dry cleaning, refrigeration and cold storage, IT 
infra, potable water, display center for local products, 
center for cultural activities etc. in a cluster of home stays.
• To create/ upgrade infrastructural facilities in the 
new/existing Industrial Areas for MSMEs.
     
• Common facilities for MSMEs to develop new products & 
processes including toolings.
• Developed infrastructure for entrepreneurs for establishing 
new units or expanding their units.
• Support for creation of common infrastructures to boost 
tourism in NER & Sikkim.

PM Vishwakarma -  Enabling Artisans & 
Craftspeople to scale-up their Enterprises.
Objective: 
• To enable the recognition of artisans and craftspeople as 
Vishwakarma, making them eligible to avail all the 
benefits under the Scheme.
• To provide skill upgradation to hone their skills and make 
relevant and suitable training opportunities available to 
them.
• To provide support for better and modern tools to 
enhance their capability, productivity, and quality of 
products and services.
• To provide the beneficiaries an easy access to collateral 
free credit and reduce the cost of credit by providing 
interest subvention.
• To provide incentives for digital transactions to 
encourage digital empowerment of Vishwakarmas.
• To provide a platform for brand promotion and market 
linkages to help them access new opportunities for 
growth.
• Recognition of artisans and craftspeople through PM 
Vishwakarma certificate and ID card.
Skill Upgradation: Basic Training of 5-7 days and 
Advanced Training of 15 days or more, with a stipend of 
Rs. 500 per day and transport allowance of Rs 1,000.
Toolkit Incentive:  A toolkit incentive of up to Rs. 15,000 
through e-vouchers.
•
•
Credit Support:  Collateral free ‘Enterprise Development 
Loans’ of upto Rs. 3  lakh in two tranches of Rs. 1 lakh and 
Rs. 2 lakh with tenures of 18 months and 30 months, 
respectively, at a concessional rate of interest fixed at 5%, 
with Government of India subvention to the extent of 8%.
•

Detailed Information: 
Tool Rooms and Technical 
Institutions 
Objective: 
• Tool Room & Technical Institutions are concentrated on 
an integrated Development of the relevant sector of 
industries to help MSME. Total 18 MSME Tool Rooms & 
Technical institutions established PAN India serving in the 
relevant sector like General Engineering, foundry & 
forging, electronics, fragrance, glass, sports good and 
footwear etc.
• Improves access of MSMEs to tooling facilities for 
enhancement of their efficiency and providing industry 
ready manpower by conducting training programme.
• Process and Product development in relevant sector.
• Consultancy and job works in relevant sector.
     
Who can apply:
Key Benefit:
• Industrial Units (focusing on MSME sector).
• The intake eligibility for the training programmes from 
school dropout to M. tech level.
Under Tool Rooms & Technical Institutions Scheme, the 
Ministry of MSME has set up 18 Tool Rooms & Technical 
Institutions {generally known as Technology Centres (TCs)} 
from 1967 to 1999 works on self-sufficiency basis in the 
sectors like General Engineering, Automation, Hand Tools, 
Plastics, Auto parts, Electrical & Electronics, Forging & 
Foundry, Sports Goods, Leather & Footwear, Fragrance & 
Flavours, etc  to support Industries for Design, Development 
and Manufacturing of Tools, Dies, Moulds, Jigs & Fixture, etc. 
MSME SCHEMES 36  For more information and regular updates, visit: www.msme.gov.in
Detailed Information: 
How to apply: 
  
• https://www.dcmsme.gov.in/CLCS_TUS_Scheme/Tool_
Room_Tech_Institutions/Scheme_Guidelines.aspx or 
website of concerned Tool Room and Technical. 
MSME SCHEMES 37  For more information and regular updates, visit: www.msme.gov.in  
• The Ministry has been investing periodically to upgrade 
the TRs & TIs by introducing new technologies such as 3D 
Printing, CAD/ CAM, CNC machining for tooling, vacuum 
heat treatment, Robotics and Process Automation etc.
• Some TCs extending support to MSMEs for complex tools, 
parts and components and also supported to the 
strategic sectors such as Defence, Aerospace etc.
• In addition, these TCs provides technical services such 
as design of parts and components, materials testing, 
heat treatment, quality control, and technical 
consultancy related to the product & process 
improvement. 
• All the TRs & TIs (TCs) adhere to the principles of Total 
Quality Management (TQM). They are ISO 9001-2015 
certified institutions and a few of them are ISO-14000, 
OHSAS-18000, ISO-29990 and ISO-50001 certified. 
Central Tool Room & Training Centre, Bhubaneswar is 
also AS-9100 certified for Aero-Space Component 
Supply.
• Apart from the technological development activities, 
TCs also provides skilling to youth for industry ready 
manpower and re-skilling the industry workforce as per 
need of industry.  TCs are conducting various training 
Programme includes NSQF Compliance Courses, 
AICTE/NCVET/SCVT approved courses and variety of 
training courses as per needs of industry.  Some TCs are 
also  conducting training programmes for international 
participants.

MSME Champions Scheme
Ministry of MSME had been implementing Credit Linked Capital Subsidy 
and Technology Upgradation Scheme (CLCS-TUS) for promoting 
competitiveness amongst Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs) 
by the way of wastage reduction through Lean Manufacturing, support for 
Design improvement, building awareness on Intellectual Property Rights, 
Zero Defect Zero Effect (ZED) Scheme, digitally empowerment of MSME 
through Digital MSME and to promote & support untapped creativity of 
individual and to promote adoption of latest technologies in 
manufacturing as well as knowledge based innovation MSMEs through 
Incubation across India.
MSME Champions scheme has been formulated through Standing Finance 
Committee (SFC) by merging all 6 components of erstwhile Technology 
Upgradation Scheme (TUS) for a period of 5 years i.e. 2021-22 to 2025-26. It 
is a holistic approach to unify, synergize and converge various schemes 
and interventions with a single purpose. The end objective is to pick up 
clusters and enterprises and modernize their processes, reduce wastages, 
sharpen business competitiveness and facilitate their National and Global 
reach and excellence. There are 3 components under the new MSME 
Champions scheme, the details of which are as below:
MSME-Sustainable (ZED)1) 
2) 
3) MSME-Competitive (Lean) 
4) Digital MSME Yet to be launched.
MSME-Innovative(for Incubation, IPR, Design)
(The Digital MSME will be interlinked with all the other 
components of the MSME Champions Scheme.)
MSME SCHEMES 38For more information and regular updates, visit: www.msme.gov.in
     
About the scheme:
Objective: 
The ZED Certification envisages promotion of Zero Defect 
Zero Effect (ZED) practices amongst MSMEs so as to:
• Encourage and enable MSMEs for manufacturing of 
quality products using latest technology, tools & to 
constantly upgrade their processes for achievement of 
high quality and high productivity with the least effect on 
the environment.
• Develop an Ecosystem for ZED Manufacturing in MSMEs, 
for enhancing competitiveness and enabling exports.
• Promote adoption of ZED practices and recognizing the 
efforts of successful MSMEs.
• Encourage MSMEs to achieve higher ZED Certification 
levels through graded incentives.
• Increase public awareness on demanding Zero Defect 
and Zero Effect products through the MSME Sustainable 
(ZED) Certification.
• Identify areas to improve upon, thereby assisting the 
Government in policy decisions and investment 
prioritization.
• Cost of Certification
• Certification Level 1: BRONZE: Rs. 8,000/-
• Certification Level 2: SILVER: Rs. 32,000/-
• Certification Level 3: GOLD: Rs. 72,000/-
• Subsidy on cost of ZED certification:
• Joining Reward of Rs. 10,000/- (Bronze will become 
free if availed)
• 80%-60%-50% for Micro, Small & Medium Enterprises
• 100% subsidy for women-owned MSMEs
     
MSME SCHEMES 39For more information and regular updates, visit: www.msme.gov.in
1)  MSME SUSTAINABLE (ZED) CERTIFICATION

• Additional subsidy:
• 10% for SC/ST owned MSMEs OR MSMEs in 
NER/Himalayan/LWE/Island territories/Aspirational 
districts.
• 5% for MSMEs which are also a part of the SFURTI OR 
Micro & Small Enterprises - Cluster Development 
Programme (MSE-CDP) of the Ministry.
• Financial Assistance in Testing/Quality/Product 
Certification:
• Up to 75% of the total cost of Testing/Certification, with 
the maximum ceiling of subsidy being Rs. 50,000/-.
• Handholding Support :
• Up-to Rs.2 lakhs for consultancy for all ZED certified 
MSMEs.
• Support in Technology Upgradation for Zero Effect 
Solutions:
• Graded incentives:
• MSMEs can avail graded incentives as prescribed for 
the three ZED Certification Levels. Wherever possible, 
the incentives provided by States will be linked 
through API integration with the ZED portal to ensure 
interoperability.
• Up-to Rs. 3 lakhs for all ZED certified MSMEs.
Scheme applicable for: 
• All MSMEs registered with the UDYAM registration portal   
(of the MoMSME) will be eligible to participate in MSME 
Sustainable (ZED) Certification and avail related 
benefits/incentives.
MSME SCHEMES 40For more information and regular updates, visit: www.msme.gov.in
  
• MSME Sustainable (ZED) Certification is an extensive drive 
to create awareness amongst MSMEs about Zero Defect 
Zero Effect (ZED) practices and motivate and incentivize 
them for ZED Certification while also encouraging them to 
become MSME Champions. Through the journey of ZED 
Certification, MSMEs can reduce wastages substantially, 
increase productivity, enhance environmental 
consciousness, save energy, optimally use natural 
resources, expand their markets, etc. MSMEs will also be 
motivated to adopt best practices in work culture, 
standardization of products, processes and systems etc. in 
order to enhance their global competitiveness and 
sustainability. The ZED Certification aims at enhancing the 
competitiveness of an MSME by assessment, modification 
through guidance, handholding, managerial and 
technological intervention – not just certification.
